observations.recommendation: In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive consider to fill-up the vacant positions, especially the department head positions, in compliance with Section 443 of RA 7160 to optimize the utilization of approved budget for Personal Services so that funds shall be utilized for its intended purpose. Initially, require the Acting- HRMO to publish vacant positions in the newspaper as required by the Civil Service Commission, wherein a copy be furnished to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: Management will strictly comply with the provision of RA 7160.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 12; Page No. 60

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant effect adjustment in the government equity account for all depreciable assets which were not provided with depreciation from CY 2020 to CY 2021 to adjust the balances thereof. Likewise, depreciation must be computed for all the properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the FS.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require:
The Municipal Accountant to set up RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year in compliance with Section 248 of RA No. 7160 and to record collections pursuant to Section 20, Volume I of NGAS for LGUs. Moreover, subsidiary ledgers pertaining to RPT and SET Receivables must be prepared and maintained; and
The Treasury Office to intensify its tax collecting scheme by posting the Notice of Delinquency in the Payment of RPT at the main entrance of Municipal Hall and other publicly accessible place and conspicuous place in each barangay as required under Section 254 of RA No. 7160 or mailing demand letters to its constituents individually in order to compel them to settle their tax liabilities.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Create an Inventory Committee that would facilitate conduct of inventory-taking of the Agency’s assets pursuant to Section 124, Volume I of NGAS Manual for LGUs. The RPCPPE must be submitted not later than January 31 of each year to the Office of the Auditor, and the same must be reconciled with the records of the Accountant; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to properly maintain Stock Cards, Property Cards, SLC and PPELC and conduct periodic reconciliation of property and accounting records to come up with accurate reports.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the balances of the Cash in Bank accounts between bank and book records by preparing the monthly BRS pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011. Balance of the SEDIP-TF account must be evaluated due to different recorded balances identified in the GF and TF books.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.

We reiterated that Management facilitate the request for write-off of the aforementioned dormant accounts duly supported by relevant documents validating the existence of the conditions. A request can be made to balances with available supporting records at present. The Audit Team will decide on the request for authority to write off for amounts not exceeding ₱100,000.00 per AO. Records for amounts exceeding ₱100,000.00 will be forwarded to the Regional Director for review and appropriate action.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by reviewing thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies during each month and ensure that remittances are based on the actual payroll deductions. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyze every transaction before recording them in the books and assign personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure submission of paid DVs on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month, and avoid voluminous submissions of DVs pertaining to transactions for several months all at once;
Evaluate the process of preparing the DVs and necessary documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation; and
Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, as amended by Section 41 of RA No. 10951, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to immediately trace all available documents and reconcile their records to come up with the correct unliquidated balance. While reconciliation is on-going, no additional CA must be granted to avoid accumulation thereof except for salaries and wages; and
Immediately send demand letters to AOs who have unsettled balances whether connected or not connected with the LGU.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Legislative body to enact immediately appropriation ordinance approving supplemental budget for the excess revenue generated by the LGU. The additional revenue can be used to finance other programs and activities of the LGU;
Ensure that the LFC perform its functions during budget preparation phase to attain reliable estimates of income to be used for the timely implementation of programs and activities; and
Continue its satisfactory efforts in the collection of income from local sources.

We recommended that Management require the concerned official to provide the Auditor at the beginning of the year a list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year to enable the former to monitor the status of its implementation and assess the Agency’s compliance to transparent and full disclosure governance.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the concerned official to submit copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders and their complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five working days after execution or issuance, for auditorial review and technical evaluation by COA TAS.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the approved AIP for 20 percent DF be submitted to DILG and DBM prior to implementation of the projects to facilitate evaluation thereof, if the same were intended for socio-economic development purposes;
Continue their progressive efforts in optimally utilizing the 20 percent DF in the implementation of development projects for the benefit of the constituents in the locality; and
Require the concerned personnel in charge of preparing the AIP to ensure that expenditures charged against the 20 percent DF are classified under CO and not MOOE.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of granting claims for procured goods and services on a reimbursement basis. Procurement of goods, infrastructure projects and consultancy services must pass through the regular mode of transparent and competitive bidding pursuant to RA No. 9184; and
Ensure that only items included in the APP will be purchased, or if there is any revision thereat, a supplemental investment program indicating additional items to be procured, must be prepared.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Strictly comply with the rules and regulations on fuel consumption to minimize wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures through usage of RIS or its equivalent, and properly accomplished driver’s trip ticket in order to ensure that government vehicles are used reasonably and to avoid audit suspension or disallowance thereof;
Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the Commission on Audit (thru Municipal Accountant) for verification and evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month; and
Allocate a reasonable budget for fuel, oil and lubricant expenses and ensure that disbursements pertinent thereto are within the limits of the appropriation.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that FTs to Barangays are supported with MOA and require them to comply with the guidelines before processing the claims and releasing the checks;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect correcting entry to set up receivable accounts while liquidation reports of the fund transfers are not yet submitted by the IAs; and
Send demand letters to the five Barangays for the immediate liquidation of the FTs.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to subject the project to public bidding and facilitate immediate implementation thereof. Management was reminded of the letter from the Bureau of Treasury per NADA dated February 26, 2021, that in case the funds remain unutilized as of December 31, 2022, it must be reverted back to the National Treasury; and
Transfer the fund to the TF bank account of the Municipality to avoid its possible misuse.

We recommended that Management:
Require the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP annually. It must present the 30 percent allocation for QRF in lump-sum and the allocation for disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness with details as to projects and activities to be funded. The same must also include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;
Require the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMF utilization report in accordance with the required format cited in COA Circular 2012-002 and promptly submit the same to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of the month. The fund must be regularly monitored and evaluated to achieve optimal utilization for the benefit of the constituents; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately transfer the unutilized QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of CY 2021 to the STF. Management is reminded that unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRMF CO must be made continuing in the GF books until the projects are completed.

We recommended that Management, through the Legislative and Human Resource Department, enact or approve resolution that would facilitate immediate creation and hiring of two additional positions in the LDRRMO as prescribed under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC 2014-1. Likewise, the head of the LDRRMO must ensure that his personnel perform their designated functions to achieve optimal implementation of LDRRM PPAs.

We recommended that Management and LSB:
Immediately prepare and submit the SEF Utilization Report for CY 2021 to the oversight agencies for monitoring, transparency and accountability in the allocation and utilization of the fund. Timelines in the preparation and submission of the required reports cited in Section 6.1 of the above-cited circular must always be observed; and
Ensure that the prescribed planning and budgeting tools are effectively utilized to attain optimal and timely SEF budget.

Except for loans and other exemptions provided fewer than 4.0 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, we recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant be advised that accounts which have been confirmed to be non-moving for ten (10) years or more be written-off. In the absence of subsidiary ledgers or transaction documents that will prove the dormancy of accounts, Management may consider to present or submit audited financial statements at least from CY 2012 to CY 2021 showing that said accounts were non-moving for at least ten (10) years.   When these accounts are confirmed to be dormant, ensure that guidelines and procedures on the write-off of accounts under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 be strictly followed.  Addressing the issue on dormant accounts will result in a fair presentation of receivable accounts in the financial statements.
We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant send Confirmation and Demand Letters requiring the creditors to pay immediately and submit accountability report for the loans received.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Acting Municipal Treasurer in cooperation with the Municipal Assessor to conduct the following:
Tax mapping and pruning of data/records relative to real properties; and
Reassessment of the value of houses, buildings, improvements on the land and/or the building, and machinery.  This will result in the increase of income from RPT and SET.
The conduct of activities mentioned above will eventually facilitate the updating of the RPTAR/Taxpayer’s Index Card, thus enable the Acting Municipal Treasurer to provide correct and reliable basis for the setting up of RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year pursuant to Section 20 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs).

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to maintain Property Ledger Card and Property Cards, respectively, which are necessary to support the accuracy and reliability of the balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
Likewise, to address this perennial issue on doubtful presentation of PPE account in the financial statement, we strongly recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Property Officer,  the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Inventory/Disposal  Committee to comply strictly with   COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the one-time cleansing of PPE account  of the LGU in order to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Assessor, in coordination with the Acting Municipal Treasurer, to produce all required documents necessary in the application for transfer and registration of ownership with the Land Registration Authority. Compliance with this recommendation is hereby enjoined to protect the interest of the LGU for any adverse claims by third parties on these properties. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds to cover all expenses to be incurred for land registration.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasury Office in coordination with the Municipal Accounting Office provide additional data on the mode of acquisition of 20 parcels of land whether these were acquired through purchase or donation. Purchase price or acquisition cost be disclosed to substantiate the reported amount of land account in the financial statement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to transfer the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱2,445,552.18 to the Trust Fund under Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years;
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to submit written explanation/ justification why the amount of ₱1,645,339.68 was recorded as Transfers of Underspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Fund account but the check issued for such transfer of funds was not actually transferred to the Trust Fund books; and
Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the details of the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF under the General Fund in the total amount of ₱313,569.01 and to transfer the said amount to Trust Fund books, to maintain subsidiary ledger for each year to facilitate the monitoring of the balance of the LDRRMF and to make disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to record purchases of supplies and materials as inventory by using the perpetual inventory method except for purchases out of petty cash fund which is to be recorded as an outright expense. Issuance of supplies and materials shall be supported by a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which will be consolidated in a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to be the basis of the OIC-Municipal Accountant to recognize the issuance of supplies in the books; and
Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies and materials semi-annually and prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). The report shall be submitted to this Office not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommend that Management explain and justify the following:
There is only one bidder in  all procurements conducted by the LGU.
All contracts for infrastructure projects were almost awarded to one contractor.
The bid of the winning bidder is almost at par with the ABC.
We also recommend that Management strictly adhere to the governing principles on government procurement, among others, the principles of transparency and competitiveness pursuant to Section 3 (a) and (b) of the 2016 IRR of RA No. 9184, to attain the overall objective of the procurement law, and that is to procure goods and services at a price most advantageous to the government. Let Management be informed that “...public officials directly or indirectly involved in the procurement process as well as in the implementation of procurement contracts and the private parties that deal with GoP are, when warranted by circumstances, investigated and held liable for their actions relative thereto.”,  as provided under Section 3 (d) of RA 9184. Non-adherence to the provisions of RA No. 9184 may lead to commit offenses and penalties under Section 65 of the same law.

We recommended that Management establish a credit line with a gasoline station nearest to the LGU to ensure that reimbursements of fuel will be avoided, except in emergency cases. Direct payment to the supplier of fuel will also ensure a collection of taxes that will accrue in favor of the government.
We further recommended that an effective internal control be established in the issuance/withdrawal of fuel, oil and lubricants from the designated gasoline station to ensure that only government vehicles of the LGU shall be accommodated. On this regard, a gas slip or its equivalent shall be approved for the purchase or withdrawal of fuel. Ensure that the plate no. of government vehicle and the no. of liters shall be clearly indicated therein to support the billing statement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the HRMDO Focal Person to  provide  COA Office a copy of Position Description of Private Secretary as attached to her appointment to determine whether her designation as Chairperson of the Inspectorate Committee and In-Charge of the Contract of Service were in accordance thereto.
We also recommended that any Order signed by the LCE designating his private secretary as Chairperson of the Inspectorate Committee and In-Charge of the Contract of Service be revoked accordingly to discontinue any exercise of power and authority  not attached to her position and official function.

We strongly recommended that the Local Chief Executive consider to fill-up the vacant positions, specifically the department head positions, in compliance with Section 443 of RA 7160 for the optimum  utilization of approved budget for Personnel Services so that the allocated funds shall be utilized for its intended purpose.
With a given commitment of the LGU in previous year that vacant positions specifically the key positions be published, we recommended that a copy of publication thereof be furnished to the Office of the Auditor for validation of actions taken by management.
We also recommended that allocation of amount for the creation of new positions be supported by a concrete plans and details such as positions and rate per month, and justification for the creation of the new positions, to substantiate the budgeted amount,  and also to ensure its implementation on the year it was allocated for maximum utilization of funds.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that electronic or computerized system in assessment and collection, as well as in budgeting and accounting be considered and adopted by the LGU which is a time and cost efficient scheme in the processing of its financial transactions. It will also facilitate the preparation of reports which are more accurate and reliable. Furthermore, it will prevent/minimize errors and inconsistencies in the assessment and collection of taxes and other revenues of the Municipality, whether these inconsistencies are intentional or not, thus, it will improve or increase the collection of the LGU. Considering the benefits it could give to management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby enjoined.

We strongly recommended that management adopt the Online Payroll System which is  better and more effective measure of internal control on payroll disbursements. It is also time and cost efficient scheme of disbursing payroll to  employees. On top of these, it will prevent possible risk of loss such as in robbery, hold-ups and similar incidents. Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby enjoined.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Acting Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of a GSO,  to:
Ensure that all motor vehicles owned by the LGU including trucks should bear government plates or red plates. If still in process, please submit proof of application for government plates, and follow up the release of red plates accordingly.
Ensure that all motor vehicles and heavy equipment owned by the LGU be properly mark “FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY” (3 inches).  Likewise, a permanent sticker for the name and logo of the Municipality of San Jacinto be also affix on those properties. Compliance therewith can be one of the effective deterrent measures from using  government vehicles and heavy equipment for  personal use and purposes; and
Provide this Office the Engine Number, Chassis Number and Product Identification Number  (PIN) of all heavy equipment and machineries owned by the LGU for  record and control purposes; and
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive initiate the establishment of a government garage, a motor pool or its equivalent for all vehicles including trucks and heavy equipment for better safekeeping, control and monitoring.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management strictly implement policies and guidelines under COA Circular No. 77-61  for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption which included the submission of Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption at the end of every month.
In addition, the following control measures are hereby recommended:
Except in emergency cases, under no circumstance that a government motor vehicles be used without the corresponding trip ticket having been duly issued by the official designated for the purpose;
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to check the completeness of Driver’s Trip Ticket in the processing of claims of fuel expenses. Ensure that duly approved Driver’s Trip Tickets are properly filled up by disclosing all necessary information about the travel to support the reasonableness and propriety of fuel consumption; and
Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer (in the absence of GSO) to ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes, and a separate file of  Driver’s Trip Tickets for each vehicle is  maintained to facilitate the preparation of the reports mentioned above.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management:
Create a position for the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO) to carry out effectively the implementation of programs, projects and activities related to Solid Waste Management to address all issues on solid waste;
Require the Municipal Planning & Development Coordinator Officer to prepare the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan which shall be submitted to the DENR for approval, and provide sanitary landfill for the disposal of collected wastes to avoid open dumpsite; and
Enjoin the barangay officials regarding the information dissemination on the importance of segregations of waste at source in their barangays in order to have a  health hazard-free environment.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to properly present the adjusted balances of each affected component of net assets/ equity by restating the corresponding prior year balances of the affected accounts shown as corresponding figures of the Financial Statements for the current year in compliance with the requirements of International Public Sector Accounting Standards No. 3.

We recommended that Management require the current Municipal School Board to reorganize the composition of the School Board and to include the duly elected representative of the Teacher’s Organization and non-academic personnel of public schools for proper representation of teachers in the Municipality.

We recommended that Management identify the cause of delay on the submission of accounts to the COA Office, and try to come up with workable solution that will effectively deal with the said issue which has been a perennial problem of the LGU.  Being intentional to address this issue  will ensure  timely submission of accounts and reports on or before the 10th day of the following month pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that Management exert concerted effort to collect the delinquent realty taxes by applying Sections 254, 256, 257 258  of RA No. 7160.  For long outstanding delinquent accounts, Management consider to impose civil remedies pursuant to  Section 174, Chapter IV of R.A. 7160 to enforce and intensity the collection of these accounts in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the LGU.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to collect immediately the delinquent market stall rentals which has been long overdue for a period of one (1) year to 19 years, otherwise, the contract shall be automatically terminated through the Municipal Mayor upon the recommendation of the Market Board, and open the said properties to the public for lease  pursuant to Item No. 3 of the Contract for Lease.

We recommended that Management submit documents that support that the foregoing projects were constructed in the government or public lot to establish the legality of project implementation, otherwise, the transaction shall be disallowed in audit. For the succeeding implementation of infrastructure projects of the LGU, one of the most important considerations to make by Management is to ensure that projects shall be implemented on a public property pursuant to the aforecited provisions of RA 7160.

We recommended that Management:
Present basis for the identification of barangay beneficiaries in the implementation of development projects and programs of the LGU.
Submit an excerpt of the Local Development Investment Plan and Comprehensive Development Plan to support that projects were included therein.
Submit a list of infrastructure projects actually implemented in the barangays of San Jacinto from 2016 to 2021. Please indicate the name of the projects, exact location (sitio/zone and barangay), and project cost.
We recommended that infrastructure projects like the farm-to-market road, multi-purpose drying pavement, and other structures that would be of much help to the constituents of San Jacinto be primarily implemented in the barangays that needed improvement and development the most, thus be given priority to be included in the preparation or revision of the Local Development Plan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System to:
Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the LCPC:
Implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues for immediate attention and action; and
Instruct members of the LCPC to prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries to fully achieve the objectives of the law creating the fund.

We recommended that  the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management (MDRRM) Officer to ensure that the LDRRMF shall be used to support disaster risk reduction and management projects and activities in compliance with the provisions of DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 13-1 and other pertinent provisions of related laws and its implementing rules and regulations in order to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to build the disaster resilience of communities, institutionalize measures for reducing disaster risks and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities in the locality; and
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to stop the practice of charging expenditures against the LDRRMF which are not directly supportive in the implementation of disaster risk management programs and activities, and to provide appropriation for the MOOE for administrative expenses of MDRRM Office.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Officer (MDRRMO) to ensure that unexpended balances of LDRRMF in previous years are included in the Revised Annual Investment Plan duly approved by the Local Sanggunian and Local Chief Executive which shall serve as the basis in the utilization thereof; and
Require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to prepare and formulate the disaster risk and reduction management programs/projects/activities of the unexpended balances of LDRRMF in previous years which shall be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.  The LDRRMFIP shall also include under a separate caption for the list of projects and activities recommended to be charged to the unexpended balances of LDRRMF in previous years.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the required BRS, pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011, and prepare Subsidiary Ledgers (SLs) per bank accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously the cash books and the data reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of the bank reconciliation statements to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant committed to a timely preparation and submission of the required BRS with request for three (3) things needed to facilitate such, namely: 1. BANK STATEMENTS - The LGU's depository/servicing banks i.e. DBP and LBP to regularly and promptly furnish the Local Accountant with the Bank Statements (BS) including debit/credit memos (DM/CM), etc., within five (5) days after the end of each month which shall be the basis for the preparation of the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements., 2. UPDATED CASH BOOK BALANCES -  It is emphasized that in preparing for the Bank Reconciliation Statements, the basis must be the Municipal Treasurer's updated cashbook balance which is reconciled with the accounting records. 3. ADDITIONAL DATA/INFORMATION affecting Bank Account Balances like NFCIs, DMs, fund transfers/grant, be promptly communicated to the Accounting Office to facilitate preparation of bank reconciliation adjustments. Also, the Municipal Accountant already coordinated with the Municipal Treasurer re: COA's recommendations for proper fund transfer/deposit of LDRRMF.
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented

Delayed receipt of some bank statements due to ongoing program for digitization of bank transaction documents.

The maintenance of updated cashbooks was given less priority due to time constraints and preoccupation with other responsibilities of the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management require that an actual inventory-taking of the Inventories and PPEs of the LGU be conducted each year pursuant to Section 124, Volume I of NGAS Manual for LGUs, that the relevant RPCPPE be submitted not later than January 31 of each year to the Office of the Auditor, and that the same be reconciled with the records of the Accountant.

Further, the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant must properly maintain Stock Cards, Property Cards, SLC and PPELC and conduct periodic reconciliation of property and accounting records to come up with accurate reports.

Moreover, the Accounting Office must review available documents and prepare adjusting entry to record the issuance/consumption of supplies and materials duly supported by the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI).
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor reconvenes the Inventory Committee, the Property Officer/Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant and all department heads concerned and direct to exert coordinated efforts for an efficient LGU property management system starting with updated RPCPPE and RCPI. Management endeavors to re-implement the 2019 Agency Action Plan, evaluate Actions Taken/Action to be Taken and review Reasons for Partial/Delay/Non-Implementation.

The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer committed to the tasks of maintaining property cards/records and periodic reconciliation of the same to come up with accurate and timely property reports. Upon receipt from Mun Treasurer of Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) based on Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which, as of the moment, are not being prepared at MTO, adjusting entries will be made at the Accounting Office for the proper expense recording of supplies and materials inventories.

For CY 2021, Management directs the Storekeeper, in coordination with assigned staff from the Municipal Administrator’s/Mayor’s Office, to facilitate the collection from every department of an inventory listing of all assets and PPEs on or before January 31 for submission to Inventory Committee.
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented | The Inventory Committee started conducting the physical count of PPEs. However, due to time constraints, they were not able to come up with an RCPPE. According to the newly-appointed HRMO, the proposed creation of General Services Officer GSO is not yet possible this CY 2021 but the planned filling up of the vacant position Supply Officer II can help the Storekeeper with the needed inventory of PPEs as per the audit report.

Numerous workloads of the assigned personnel precluded them from complying with the audit recommendations. 


The Municipal Accountant is still in the process of retrieving supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Management:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation expense of the PPE with available documents, using the method prescribed in the aforementioned PAGs; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to prioritize the provision of depreciation for the assets procured/constructed, review PPE schedules, and gather supporting documents attached on the affected accounts. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has already provided depreciation for the PPEs with available documents starting specifically with those procured/constructed from year 2015 to 2019. Also, depreciation expenses were already provided with most of the identified movable PPEs.  Further, review of PPE schedules/supporting documents through coordination with the LCE and other property management officials is needed for the proper identification of acquisition cost, useful life, and residual value of PPEs,i.e. like Road Networks, Flood Control Systems,Water Supply Systems, the depreciation computation of which are not specifically provided in the IPSASs/COA guidelines. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements. The Municipal Accountant acknowledged the audit observations and committed to implement the recommendation.
observations.reason: Partially Implemented | Depreciation for some of the Municipality’s PPE was already provided. However, provision of depreciation to some PPEs specifically Public Infrastructure assets were still not prioritized due to uncertainty of the estimated useful life and residual value to be used by the Accounting Office in the computation of depreciation for such assets.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.
observations.managementAction: An initial review conducted by the Municipal Accountant on the said reciprocal accounts showed that out of the ₱1,212,478.17 total variance ₱1,210,692.66 pertains to the Receivable from GF and SEF for the Share of the Province and Barangays from the RPT collections that were transferred from General Fund to the Trust Fund but without the corresponding receivable entry in the Trust fund. Due to lack of staff and time constraints since the adjustment entry involves review of  transactions from many years back as far as 2003, the Municipal  Accountant opted to first assign a personnel that will retrieve the supporting documents relative to the  mentioned reciprocal accounts,and only from then on can provide necessary adjusting entries to correct the book balances.
observations.reason: Partially Implemented | Initial review of these reciprocal accounts was conducted but final booking of adjustments in the books of account was not made possible due to the sheer volume of transactions which are processed in Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officers and all others concerned to strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of financial reports. Accordingly, appropriate remedial measures must be effected by Management to ensure that the required financial reports are submitted promptly without the need to demand from time to time.
observations.managementAction: Management recognizes the fact that prompt submission of disbursement and collection accounts by the Municipal Treasurer, appropriation/obligation reports in SAAOUB by the Municipal Budget Officer, LDRRMF Utilization Report by the MDRRMO, and other mandated reports from other LGU officials will all help facilitate timely review by the Municipal Accountant of the agency’s financial transactions, and consequently, ensure compliance with the legal deadlines for rendition of LGU accounts. Submission of Monthly Trial Balance was not implemented because the Accounting Office is the one preparing the SAAO or Status of Appropriation Allotments and Obligations  which is part of such required Monthly reports. Although preparation of Quarterly and Interim Financial Statements was not prioritized, the Office of the Municipal Accountant has exerted best efforts to fully comply with the submission timelines for Quarterly/Semestral Reports required by COA, DILG and other NGAs and For CY 2021, the Accounting Dept was able to submit the 3rd Quarter Consolidated Trial Balance as of September 30, 2021 on December 10, 2021
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented | For CY 2021, the Accounting Dept was able to submit the 3rd Quarter Consolidated Trial Balance as of September 30, 2021 on December 10, 2021


Due to lack of manpower and required report coordination among accountable officials, Management admitted the delayed/non submission of Monthly and Quarterly Trial Balance and Interim Financial Statements and supporting schedules.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure submission of paid DVs on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month, and avoid voluminous submissions of DVs pertaining to transactions for several months all at once;

Evaluate the process of preparing the DVs and necessary documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation; and

Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already assigned a staff to help implement this audit recommendation. Also, sought the cooperation of co-finance officials especially the Municipal Treasure for timely submission to the Accounting Office of DVs complete with supporting documents to facilitate compliance to COA's financial reporting and accounts submission requirements. The Management gave assurance that payments are only made to proper claims and duly processed Vouchers/Payrolls. If there are times that vouchers submission is incomplete and/or delayed it is because of  payments, though duly processed but are not acknowledged/signed on time by claimants like the Remittances of RPT Shares of Barangays, etc. For 2021, DVs and accounts for the 3rd quarter are already submitted on December 2021.
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented




Lack of manpower in the Accounting Department that would assist in sorting and organizing the DVs




Lack of staff assigned to conduct pre-audit of the disbursements





Lack of supervision and monitoring of the concerned official

observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant reported that most of these cash advances were granted several years ago and for the purpose of payroll, special TF and travel.  It can also be noted in the Accountant's regularly submitted report on Monitoring of Cash Advances that only ₱6.52 million or 19.76 per cent of the total cash advances belongs to Accountable Officers (AOs) that are still connected with the Agency, while the remaining 80.24 per cent belongs to AOs who are either dead, reassigned, resigned or reached end of term.

The LCE is seriously contemplating of taking legal action against those former officers with unliquidated cash advances. He is currently seeking legal advice on the matter.
observations.reason:
As of December 15, 2021, all current year cash advances of regular employees   are fully liquidated/settled. 


Lack of supervision and monitoring of the concerned official to implement the audit recommendation. 




Weak internal control




Other concerns precluded the Agency from complying with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exhaust all options to collect from the AOs concerned and to facilitate the request for write-off of the aforementioned account balances if collection thereof is not feasible so that the same will be derecognized in the Financial Statements, subject to the procedures and requirements set forth in COA Circular 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were again sent to Accountable Officers reminding them of their accountabilities with the LGU
observations.reason: The Office of the Municipal Accountant communicated to the Management that per LGU's Financial Statements and Reports, a huge amount of cash advance amounting to ₱15.59 million, which is already existing in the Agency’s records for the past 11 to 15 years, belongs to the former Municipal Treasurer who is now reassigned to another agency. She also added that this was always properly disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements that said account was subjected to special audit and  recommended for case filing.

The Municipal Accountant commits to fully comply  to the recommended corrective measures pending receipt from the Municipal Treasurer of the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer's Index Card or duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year which is needed for her to properly set up RPT and SET Receivables for the year.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant acknowledged that she was unable to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

Strictly comply with the rules and regulations on fuel consumption to minimize wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures through usage of RIV or its equivalent, and properly accomplished driver’s trip ticket in order to ensure that government vehicles are used reasonably and to avoid audit suspension or disallowance thereof; and

Submit the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Official Travels within 10 days of the succeeding month for validation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the:
Municipal Treasurer to transfer cash to the DRRM Bank account of the Municipality corresponding to the unexpended/unobligated balance of QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to be made available as stand-by fund for relief and recovery PPAs;

LDRRMO to ensure that the LDRRMFIP correspond to the budget appropriated for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery and to submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant on or before the 15th  day after the end of each month; and

Agency officials and employees to be more prudent in the utilization of its LDRRMF, taking into consideration the existing rules and regulations governing the utilization of the said fund.
observations.managementAction:
1) The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Municipal Budget Officer,the Municipal Treasurer and the newly appointed LDRRMO came up with the updated amount of unexpended balances of QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE to be transferred to the Trust Fund bank account with the corresponding cash back up in order to reflect the correct balances of the cash and liability accounts in the financial statements.  

 2) The Municipal Mayor endeavored to direct MDRRMC thru the  MDRRMO  to come up with well-planned/ studied LDRRMFIP and to see to it that the transferred LDRRMF will be incorporated in the LDRMMFIP; and  3) The MDRRMO and Budget Office promised to coordinate with the  Accounting Office to reconcile their records to ensure that the LDRRMF expenditures are within the budget appropriated for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.    4) The DRRMO, upon coordination with and certification of the Mun Accountant, is required to submit the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRM Fund to COA, OCD and DILG-LGOO in accordance with Section 5.1. of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.reason:
Lack of supervision and monitoring of the concerned official

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management and the LSB to:

Ensure that the prescribed planning and budgeting tools are effectively utilized in order to prepare an optimal and timely SEF Budget; and

Strictly conform to the timelines in the planning and budgeting process as prescribed under DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circular No. 2017-01.

We also recommended that the previous years’ unexpended balances be indicated in the Income and Expenditures Estimates for the budget year so that the total amount available for appropriation could be determined to ensure funding of PPAs of public schools in the municipality.

The Municiapal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Budget Officer will ensure the inclusion of the previous years' unexpended balances in the determination of the amount available for appropriation and will be indicated in the Income and Expenditures Estimates for the budget year.

Laxity in complying with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the:

Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over the remittance of Inter-Agency Payables through strict coordination with the personnel in-charge of payroll,  reconciliation between the billing statement and the payroll deductions, and ensure that remittances are based on actual collections and payroll deductions;

Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere and observe deadlines for remittances to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth in order to avoid penalties/surcharges; and

Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation and trace the cause of the abnormal negative balances of these accounts and effect the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: Variances in the Inter-agency payables are part of ongoing account reconciliation with GSIS,Pag-IBIG, Philhealth and BIR that has started many years back. The Accounting Office has been keeping up with recent and ever-changing remittance processing systems of the National Government Agencies and GOCC's. The Municipal Accountant promised to conduct a detailed review of these inter-agency accounts and will provide necessary adjustments to reconcile the balances.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the:
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the records of their offices to determine the Accountable/Liquidating Officers who have outstanding undeposited collections, trace collections already deposited but not recorded in the books, and to verify the actual accountability of the Municipal Treasurer;

Municipal Treasurer to ensure that collections from various collecting officers are remitted daily to the Liquidating Officer/s and the same are regularly deposited to the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB). The Liquidating Officer must verify the unused accountable forms in the possession of collecting clerks upon remittance to see to it that all collections are remitted daily and intact. The new booklet of accountable forms must only be issued upon full consumption of the whole stub to prevent the collecting clerks from retaining huge amount of collections in their custody; and

Municipal Accountant to record the undeposited collections as accountability of the actual persons responsible and to ensure that deposits thereof are recorded in the books.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer already started reconciliation of their respective records/books of account.  Maintenance of subsidiary ledgers in the Accounting Office are for assigned Mun.Treasurers/ Accountable officers only. Breakdown of accountability per collecting clerks was not prioritized by the Municipal Accountant due to voluminous tasks in the Accounting Office and the persistent failure of the MTO to furnish the Accounting Office with updated Inventory of Accountable Form,(i.e. The ending balance does not tally with the forwarded beginning balance plus additions minus issuances). The Municipal Treasurer should be the one primarily responsible to the monitoring of collection and deposit transactions of every Treasury staff under her supervision, even if she has assigned it to a liquidatind officer.
observations.reason: Pertinent records of the two concerned Offices are still in process of retrieval

Municipal Accountant to monitor the transactions in the trust fund books most especially fund transfers and diligently apply relevant accounting treatment thereto.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.
observations.managementAction: Since the adjustment entry involves review of  transactions as far as 2003, the Municipal  Accountant opted to first assign a personnel that will retrieve the supporting documents relative to the  mentioned reciprocal accounts.
observations.reason: Initial review of these reciprocal accounts was conducted but final booking of adjustments in the books of account was not made possible due to the sheer volume of transactions which are processed in Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by reviewing thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies during each month and ensure that remittances are based on the actual payroll deductions. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyze every transactions before recording them in the books and assign a personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant promised to conduct a detailed review of these inter-agency accounts and will provide necessary adjustments to reconcile the balances.
observations.reason: Variances in the Inter-agency payables are part of ongoing account reconciliation that has started many years back.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that Management:

Require the Municipal Accountant, the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (LDRRMO), the Budget Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to review all available documents relative to LDRRMF and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the financial statements and other documents;

Instruct the aforementioned officers to revisit and strictly follow the provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 10121, COA Circular No. 2012-002 and National Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council – Department of Budget and Management – Department of Interior and Local Government (NDRRMC-DBM-DILG) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for transfers of Agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the Special TF by year of transfer, receipts of NDRRMF by transferring agency, DRRMF from other LGUs by LGU and other sources by donor.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and all others who are involved in the preparation of the financial statements and their supporting schedules, immediately submit the required financial reports.


Strict compliance with the rules and regulations on the submission of monthly and quarterly financial reports to the Auditor within the prescribed period as required under Section 70 of NGAS Manual for LGUs and Section 122 of PD 1445 is enjoined.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Accountant has exerted best efforts to fully comply with the submission timelines.  Nevertheless, Management promised to comply deadlines on submission of disbursement and collection accounts  by the Mun. Treasurer, obligation reports by the Mun. Budget Officer, LDRRMF Utilization Report by the MDRRMO, and other mandated reports from other LGU officials.
observations.reason:
Due to lack of manpower and required report coordination among accountable officials, the management admitted the delayed/non submission of Monthly and Quarterly Trial Balance and Interim Financial Statements and supporting schedule.


Outdated records of the Accounting Office and bulk of transactions needed to be processed precluded from complying with the prompt preparation and submission of monthly and quarterly reports.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require all AOs to submit necessary documents to support their claims to the Office of the Municipal Accountant and direct the Municipal Accountant to submit all the disbursement vouchers together with their supporting documents from January to December 2019 to the Office of the Auditor;

Evaluate the process of preparing the disbursement vouchers and necessary documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation; and

Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected copies of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review.

We also directed the attention of Management to Section 4.0, the Penalty Clause, of COA Circular No. 2009-001, which states that the inability of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements of the same must be subject to administrative disciplinary action and the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents be may be suspended in audit.
observations.managementAction: On-line/internet submission will be considered for fast and timely submission of documents needed in the report.
observations.reason: The LGU being an island municipality finds it difficult to meet the 5 day reglementary period considering that most of the contractors are from Mainland Bicol, Masbate and Metro Manila.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the LDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to secure immediately the needed documents to facilitate the timely preparation of the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and submit a copy thereof to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, pursuant to the procedures of the aforecited Circular, to facilitate verification thereof.
observations.managementAction: LDRRMO will verify his records with the records of the Budget and Accounting Office and promised to submit the required report monthly.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant was able to prepare only Quarterly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF due to time constraints and the lack of coordination with the MDRRMO and Mun. Budget Officer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the LCPC to actively perform their duties and responsibilities to ensure full strengthening and implementation of the LCPC PPAs and widen the scope of the PPAs for LCPC as enumerated in Section II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 and prioritize the PPAs with long term effects/involvement on children.
observations.reason: Some of the offices concerned failed to implement its planned PPAs due to the restrictions and protocols imposed during the pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the SWM Focal Person to prioritize the approval of the MSWMP that will ensure the efficient management of solid wastes generated within the Municipality’s jurisdiction; and re-organize the MSWMB, compliant with prescribed composition enumerated in Section 16 of RA No. 9003, that will adapt measures to promote the effective implementation of the solid waste management programs.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management implement immediately the PPAs pertinent to the unutilized balance of ₱8.03 million as embodied in the Annual Investment Program (AIP). We also recommended that Management judiciously plan the PPAs to be funded out of the 20 per cent LDF and ensure its full utilization for the benefit of the constituents and local government itself, in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 2, 2017.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Advise the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions of the New Government Accounting System Manual for Local Government Units (NGAS-LGUs), COA Circular 2010-001 and IPSAS I in the submission of the complete set of financial reports on a monthly, quarterly and annual basis; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over the record keeping of the Municipality’s transactions and to devise and implement strategies that will help the Accounting Office in the performance of its duties and responsibilities.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounting Office evaluated their process and implemented additional accounting controls to improve their processes, thus CY 2020 financial statements were submitted fairly within the prescribed period with time allowance for some consolidation/revisions.

Management promised to implement more controls to strengthen accounting processes and will consider additional personnel in the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Lack of supervision and monitoring

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant conducted a detailed review of such reciprocal accounts and will provide necessary adjustments to correct the balances pending coordination with the Mun. Treasurer and/or MBO.
observations.reason: Review of these reciprocal accounts was not fully implemented due to the bulk of transactions processed both in the Accounting and Treasury Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by requiring the Municipal Accountant to review thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies during the month and that remittances must be based on the actual payroll deductions. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyze every transaction before recording in the books. Assign personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office promised to review the schedules and to establish additional controls to prevent this deficiency from happening again.
observations.reason: Review of these accounts was only partially implemented because there are accounts needing ample time and additional staff to review transactions dating many years back.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Create a General Services Officer (GSO) position that will discharge on a full time basis the property custodianship functions and the receipt, issuance and recording of the procurement and issuance of supplies and property, maintain updated Property Cards per category on all property, plant and equipment as recorded in the list of Property, Plant and Equipment of accounting records and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the RPCPPE;

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain property ledger cards consistent with their accounting records; and

Maintain and renew all Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment every three years for the signature of the holder of each item listed in the prepared RPCPPE or based on the old Memorandum Receipts on file.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to prioritize the conduct of inventory-taking and the preparation and submission of the required RPCPPE.
observations.reason: The creation of a GSO was not considered in CY 2019 because of budget issues. The Municipal Treasurer explained that the conduct of inventory-taking was not possible due to time constraint. Maintenance of PPELC was not prioritize by Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant prepares a schedule based from electronic file which lists all the recently procured PPE, the entries made therein are tallied in the amount presented in the PPE account on the Statement of Financial Position.  Because of the voluminous transactions in the office and lack of personnel to carry out the manual updating of entries in the PPE Ledger Cards that these were not maintained in the required format.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation based on available documents especially for those recently acquired properties and make the necessary adjustment on the affected accounts. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the depreciable properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management will conduct further review of PPE schedules/supporting docs, and coordinate with the LCE and other property management officials relative to proper identification of acquisition cost, useful life, and residual value of PPEs specifically Public Infrastructure assets.
observations.reason: Depreciation for some of the Municipality’s PPE was already provided. However, provision of depreciation to some PPEs specifically Public Infrastructure assets were still not prioritized due to uncertainty of the estimated useful life and residual value to be used by the Accounting Office in the computation of depreciation for such assets.

Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasury Office to implement collection strategies to further improve revenue assessment and collection.

We also recommended that the Sanguniang Bayan devise an ordinance that will assist the Treasury Office in assessing the basis for CTC tax, such as requiring the CTC taxpayers to file a sworn declaration of total earnings derived from business, compensation, exercise of profession and income from real property during the preceding year.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality:

Revisit the provisions of the CY 2009 Revenue Code with the end-in-view of considering the call of the present times towards the best interest of the public and the government; and

Update the existing tax rates, fees and charges prescribed in the Revenue Code to be able to sustain an adequate, appropriate and relevant tax rates in relation to the Municipality’s fast-paced growth and development requirements.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation:
Considering the material effect of the doubtful accuracy, validity and existence of the property, plant and equipment in the fair presentation of the financial statements of the local government unit,  we are reiterating our previous years audit recommendation which were seemingly overlooked by Management to implement. Other seven (7) audit recommendations were added to strengthen the internal control system of the LGU in property management, to wit:



Facilitate the creation on an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of  properties of the municipality. The actual count could be undertaken any month during the current  year, but it has to be updated at year-end. A copy of the Inventory Report has to be submitted to COA not later than January 31of each year. 

Reconcile the PPE per inventory report with accounting records/reports. Any discrepancies shall be verified and adjusted accordingly.  Recipient of any missing PPEs be held accountable. 


Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.


Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.


Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.


Make use of Invenory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper accountability and control. 


During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal. 

Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.



Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to certify only the loan applications of employees whose salary could accommodate the monthly loan amortization and would still have a take home pay of not less than the amount specified in the General Provisions of the General Appropriation Act.
