observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous years’ audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to locate the records of the receivables account to determine the details as to name of individuals/debtors and government agencies and nature of the receivables and to maintain subsidiary ledgers for all receivables account to substantiate its balance and effect collection of the receivables from them.
We also recommended that accounts which have been confirmed to be non-moving for ten (10) years be written-off because these are already considered as dormant accounts. Ensure that guidelines and procedures on the write-off of accounts provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 be strictly followed.

We recommended that the Local Executive require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to furnish the OIC-Municipal Accountant the list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the current year based on Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting-up of receivables at the beginning of the year for the real property taxes and special education taxes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that:
All cash advances be liquidated at the end of the year.
All cash advances be reported as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served.
Cash advances of the municipal officials and employees amounting to ₱51,181.00 are liquidated; otherwise, withhold the salaries of the officials and employees whose cash advances remain unliquidated once efforts yield no encouraging results.

To address this perennial issue on doubtful presentation of PPE accounts in the financial statement, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Property Officer,  the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee to comply strictly with   COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts  of the LGU in order to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to adhere with the accrual method of accounting by recognizing income when earned, whether collected or not, pursuant to Paragraph 19 of IPSAS No. 9 and Section 47, Volume I of GAM for LGUs. Thus, the uncollected rental income from delinquent stallholders with a total amount of ₱1,079,740.00 be recognized in the books as debit to Operating Lease Receivable and Credit to Rent Income.
Direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to prepare a monthly billing statements for stall rentals and submit a summary of the same to the OIC-Municipal Accountant for recording accrued income in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting or correcting entries to record the 15% share of the Office of the Building Official (OBO) from collections of Building Permit fees as Trust Liabilities under Trust Fund. Strict adherence of the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units is further recommended for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare journal entry vouchers to recognize the transfer of the Share from Tobacco Excise Tax from Trust Fund to the General Fund.
Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that Share from Tobacco Excise Tax be appropriated accordingly prior to the utilization of the said fund; and
Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to furnish the Audit Team copy of the list of programs and projects to be implemented supported by the approved Sanggunian Ordinance or resolution, mechanism and period of implementation relative to the Share Tobacco Excise Tax received as required in Sections 2.5, 2.6 and 3.2 of Local Budget Memorandum No. 76 of DBM dated October 6, 2017.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterate the previous years’ audit recommendations that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare journal entry vouchers to recognize the transfer of the Share from PCSO from Trust Fund to the General Fund.
Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that Share from PCSO be appropriated accordingly prior to the utilization of the said fund; and
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit copies of Memorandum of Agreement entered into between the LGU and PCSO for CYs 2018, 2019 and 2020 indicating the category of programs and projects including the amount of allocation or funds and the reportorial requirements pertaining to its utilization as stated in Section 40 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the Small Town Lottery Operations of the PCSO.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to record purchases of supplies and materials as inventory by using the perpetual inventory method except for purchases out of petty cash fund which is to be recorded as an outright expense. Issuance of supplies and materials shall be supported by a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which will be consolidated in a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to be the basis of the OIC-Municipal Accountant to recognize the issuance of supplies in the books.
Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies and materials semi-annually and prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). The report shall be submitted to this Office not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommended that the Accounting Unit exhaust all possible means to determine the details and dates of acquisition of the equipment recorded under the PPE account. We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant provide depreciation cost and record the same in the books of accounts to reflect the correct carrying value of the PPE account in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous years’ audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Assessor, in coordination with the Acting Municipal Treasurer, to cause the immediate retrieval of all necessary documents of ownership of the parcels of land owned by the LGU and submit application for transfer and registration of ownership with the Land Registration Authority to establish absolute ownership in conformity with Section 39 of PD No. 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of GAAM in order to protect the interest of the LGU for any adverse claims by third parties.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds that will cover the expenses on the registration of parcels of land.

We recommended that Management:
Submit a MOA or contract to establish authority of the LGU to use or make any modification on the structure of the private building out of government funds, and also to bind the private owner and the LGU on terms and conditions in favor of the government, otherwise, the expenditure shall be considered as illegal or irregular expenditure.
Avoid stating several purposes of budget in one project like “Construction/Repair/ Lease/Rental of additional space/ building” which is vague. The purpose of the budget should be specific and clear.
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entry in the recognition of expenses incurred in the conversion of private building into a quarantine facility with a total amount of ₱1,000,000.00 which has been recognized as Repair and Maintenance- Buildings and Other Structures in the books. Recognition of expenditure be based on the MOA.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the lacking documents to the Audit Team pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 to ensure the validity and propriety of the foregoing transactions.
We also recommended that Management instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer, with the assistance of the OIC-Municipal Accountant, to require the regular employees and JOWs concerned to refund the overpayment of hazard pay.

We recommended that Management:
Require the MSWD Officer, in coordination with the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer, to submit copy of Social Amelioration Card (SAC) Forms, copy of valid IDs, and pictures of beneficiaries during actual receipt of financial assistance for further evaluation of the Audit Team.
Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to submit justification/s regarding the payout of financial subsidy to the 182 beneficiaries who did not affix their signatures on the receipt portion of the payroll for evaluation by the Audit Team.
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to adhere to the use of proper account titles in line with the description prescribed in the Chart of Accounts for LGUs to ensure that accounts were properly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that  Management stop the practice of using government funds to pay  legal services of a private lawyer, and seek legal assistance instead from the Provincial Legal Office or Prosecutor’s Office pursuant to the provisions of Section 481 of RA No. 7160 and Revised Guidelines on the Deputation of Private Lawyers and Special Attorneys dated December 15, 2015.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretary, Municipal Engineer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents enumerated therein to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction.
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents in the processing of payments of disbursement vouchers.
Require the BAC and TWG to submit a written explanation/s regarding the deficiencies noted and reason on awarding contract to a contractor without a PCAB Registration.
Require the BAC and TWG to submit written justification/s on deficiencies noted and the OIC-Municipal Accountant on the recording of the payment of 15% mobilization as Construction in Progress account instead of Advances to Contractors account for further evaluation by the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management stop the charging previous year’s expenditures to current appropriation pursuant to Section 119 of PD 1445. We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant set a deadline for the submission of all claims of officials and employees, and creditors before the closing of accounts at the end of the year in order to obligate expenditures on the year they actually incurred.

We recommended that policies and guidelines under COA Circular No. 77-61 be given due regard for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption which included the submission of Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption at the end of every month. Prescribed format of said reports are provided for ready reference.
In addition, the following measures are hereby recommended:
For motor vehicle which is not exclusively used by a government official, it is suggested that each time the vehicle is used, require that the vehicle be returned  in the office  full tank, thus the actual no. of liters consumed for each travel is immediately determined and the same shall be indicated in the Driver’s Trip Ticket; and
Ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes, and maintain a separate file of  Driver’s Trip Ticket for each vehicle for easy monitoring.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Personnel Officer/HRMO and OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Submit the DTRs of all Public Health Workers and other personnel concerned to support the validity of the claims on hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances in CY 2020.
Observe the rules and regulations in granting hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances, and to ensure that all claims are properly documented pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001, and DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1, S, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
Create a position for the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO) to carry out effectively the implementation of programs, projects and activities related to Solid Waste Management to address all issues on solid waste;
Require the Municipal Planning & Development Coordinator Officer to prepare the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan which shall be submitted to the DENR for approval, and provide sanitary landfill for the disposal of collected wastes to avoid open dumpsite; and
Enjoin the barangay officials regarding the information dissemination on the importance of segregations of waste at source in their barangays in order to provide a pollution and health hazard-free environment.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to properly present the adjusted balances of each affected component of net assets/ equity by restating the corresponding prior year balances of the affected accounts shown as corresponding figures for the current year Financial Statements in compliance with the requirements of International Public Sector Accounting Standards No. 3.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to ensure that claims on RATA be supported by the following:
Certification from the Office of the HRMO showing the number of workdays of actual performance in a month duly supported by approved DTRs for officials who adopt a work schedule of eight hours per day, forty hours per week.
Certification by the SB Secretary showing the actual work/attendance in a month duly supported by a certified copy of attendance sheet on regular sessions for officials who, by the nature of their official functions, adopt a work schedule other than the usual eight hours per day, forty hours per week.
We also recommended that the foregoing documents be submitted to support the payment of RATA in CY 2020.

We recommended that management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to release the shares of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the provision of Section 271 of Republic Act 7160 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for development projects which are beneficial to the barangay folks.

We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and approve by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and latest GPPB issuances.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions.

We reiterate the previous years audit recommendations that the Management exert concerted effort to collect delinquent realty taxes by applying Section 254 of RA 7160.  For long outstanding delinquent accounts, Management may consider to impose civil remedies as provided under Section 174, Chapter IV of R.A. 7160 to enforce and intensity the collection of these accounts in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the LGU.
We also recommended that the actual amount delinquent accounts per list or schedule submitted by the Municipal Treasury Office be reconciled with the books of accounts maintained by the Municipal Accounting Office.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort to collect the delinquent market stall rentals from stallholders by adopting an effective collection strategy in order to generate more income which could be used in the implementation of development programs/projects beneficial to the constituents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive has to ensure adherence with existing laws, rules and regulations on the remittance of mandatory contributions/premiums to GSIS, and PhilHealth by requiring the OIC-Municipal Accountant to conduct a detailed review and analysis of the premiums and contributions withheld from salaries of municipal officials and employees, and remittances thereof, and if warranted, make the corresponding adjustments.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant, in cooperation of the Acting Municipal Treasurer, to cause the immediate remittance of the premiums/contributions totaling ₱1,710,468.19 to the GSIS and PhilHealth to ensure that municipal officials and employees will enjoy the services and benefits offered by the said government institutions and to avoid the interest, penalties and surcharges that may be imposed for non-compliance.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues, promote women’s empowerment, and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, and to charge these expenses to other programs of the LGU, where deemed appropriate.
Require the GAD Focal Point to determine GAD related issues in the locality which is necessary in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.
Ensure the preparation of GAD Accomplishment Report which should be submitted on January or February of the succeeding year to support and document the implementation of GAD- related projects, programs and activities as stated in the GAD Plan and Budget in compliance with the provisions of C.8 and 5.5 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.

We recommended that Management require the Local School Board to include allocations from its SEF in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC):
Implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues that need immediate attention and action.
Require the members of the LCPC to prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries to fully achieve the objectives of the law creating the fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to transfer the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱1,216,346.71 under the General Fund to the Trust Fund under Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to restore the total amount of ₱731,169.42 under the General Fund and transfer the amount of ₱582,984.42 to Trust Liabilities-DRMMF account under the Trust Fund while the balance of ₱148,185.00 shall be recognized in the books as Continuing Appropriation-Capital Outlay  under LDRRMF in the SCBAA of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that prior year’s unexpended balances of LDRRMF are included in the Revised Annual Investment Plan duly approved by the local sanggunian and Local Chief Executive which shall serve as the basis in the utilization thereof.
Require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to prepare and formulate the disaster risk and reduction management programs/projects/activities of the previous year’s balances of LDRRMF which shall be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.  The LDRRMFIP shall also include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities recommended to be charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that management adopt the Online Payroll System which is a time and cost efficient scheme of disbursing payroll to its employees. Likewise, it prevents possible risk such as robbery, hold-up and similar incidents. Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby encourage.

We reiterate our audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive:
Create a committee to undertake a thorough study and evaluation on the actual workloads and manpower requirements of the Municipality for each Department/Office to maximize the services of Job Order Personnel, thus, minimize government spending. Based on the study and evaluation, trimmed down the number of JOs to what is really required and necessary in the operation of the Municipality in order to maximize the use of government funds.
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to require the submission of accomplishment reports of Job Order Personnel as basis for payment of wages.
Require the Acting HRMO to submit copies of contract of services of Job Order Workers to support the payment of wages.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that electronic or computerized system in assessment and collection be considered and adopted by the LGU of San Jacinto which is a time and cost efficient scheme in processing transactions. It will also facilitate the preparation of reports which are accurate and reliable. Furthermore, it will prevent/minimize errors and inconsistencies in the assessment and collection of taxes and other revenues of the Municipality, whether intentional or not, thus, it will improve or increase the collection of the LGU. Considering the benefits it could give to management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby encourage.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive consider to fill-up the vacant positions, especially the department head positions, in compliance with Section 443 of RA 7160 to optimize the utilization of approved budget for Personal Services so that funds shall be utilized for its intended purpose. Initially, require the Acting- HRMO to publish vacant positions in the newspaper as required by the Civil Service Commission, wherein a copy be furnished to the Audit Team.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the
preparation and submission of the required BRS, pursuant to COA Circular
No. 96-011, and prepare Subsidiary Ledgers (SLs) per bank accounts; and
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously the cash books and the
data reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of the bank
reconciliation statements to ensure that any reconciling items will be
accordingly adjusted.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require that an actual
inventory-taking of the Inventories and PPEs of the LGU be conducted each year
pursuant to Section 124, Volume I of NGAS Manual for LGUs, that the relevant
RPCPPE be submitted not later than January 31 of each year to the Office of the
Auditor, and that the same be reconciled with the records of the Accountant.
Further, the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant must properly maintain Stock
Cards, Property Cards, SLC and PPELC and conduct periodic reconciliation of
property and accounting records to come up with accurate reports.

Moreover, the Accounting Office must review available documents and prepare
adjusting entry to record the issuance/consumption of supplies and materials duly
supported by the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI).

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

a) Direct the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation expense of the
PPE with available documents, using the method prescribed in the
aforementioned PAGs; and
b) Require the Municipal Accountant to prioritize the provision of depreciation
for the assets procured/constructed, review PPE schedules, and gather
supporting documents attached on the affected accounts. Henceforth,
depreciation must be computed for all the properties of the Municipality
periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts
in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant conduct a
detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled accounts.
Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to
present the correct balances of the affected accounts. Henceforth, exercise care in
recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the
balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal
Budget Officers and all others concerned to strictly comply with the rules and
regulations on the submission of financial reports. Accordingly, appropriate remedial
measures must be effected by Management to ensure that the required financial
reports are submitted promptly without the need to demand from time to time.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure submission of paid DVs on or
before the 10th day of the ensuing month, and avoid voluminous submissions
of DVs pertaining to transactions for several months all at once;
b. Evaluate the process of preparing the DVs and necessary documents to
prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be
reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they
were properly approved and with complete documentation; and
c. Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or
criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of Presidential Decree (PD) No.
1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of
the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their
inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended that Management:

a. Direct Officers and Employees with cash advances to immediately submit
their liquidation papers, otherwise, require the immediate withholding of
salary of those officers and employees who were unable to comply with the
requirement;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the liquidation of
outstanding cash advances and send written demand on the submission of
liquidation report within ten days before the thirty-day period, pursuant to
Section 5.l of COA Circular No. 97-002;
c. Stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to those who have
unliquidated cash advances unless the previous ones are fully settled; and
d. Institute the appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those erring
accountable officers/employees (AO) and be reminded that the Auditor may
order the suspension of their salaries, as cited in Section 9.2 and 9.3 of COA
Circular No. 2012-004.

We recommended that Management exhaust all options to collect from the AOs
concerned and to facilitate the request for write-off of the aforementioned account
balances if collection thereof is not feasible so that the same will be derecognized in the
Financial Statements, subject to the procedures and requirements set forth in COA
Circular 2016-005.

We recommend that Management require the:

a) Municipal Accountant to set up RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning
of the year in compliance with Section 248 of RA No. 7160 and to record
collections pursuant to Section 20, Volume I of NGAS for LGUs. Moreover,
SLs pertaining to RPT and SET Receivables must be prepared and
maintained; and
b) Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort in improving the collection
efficiency of the Municipality by posting the Notice of Delinquency in the
Payment of RPT at the main entrance of Municipal Hall and other publicly
accessible place and conspicuous place in each barangay as required under
Section 254 of RA No. 7160 or mailing demand letters to its constituents
individually in order to compel them to settle their tax liability.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

a) Strictly comply with the rules and regulations on fuel consumption to
minimize wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures through usage
of RIV or its equivalent, and properly accomplished driver’s trip ticket in
order to ensure that government vehicles are used reasonably and to avoid
audit suspension or disallowance thereof; and
b) Submit the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Official Travels
within 10 days of the succeeding month for validation.

We recommended that Management require the:

a. Municipal Treasurer to transfer cash to the DRRM Bank account of the
Municipality corresponding to the unexpended/unobligated balance of QRF
and the DRRMF-MOOE to be made available as stand-by fund for relief and
recovery PPAs;
b. LDRRMO to ensure that the LDRRMFIP correspond to the budget
appropriated for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response,
rehabilitation and recovery and to submit the Monthly Report on Sources and
Utilization of the DRRMF duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant
on or before the 15th day after the end of each month; and
c. Agency officials and employees to be more prudent in the utilization of its
LDRRMF, taking into consideration the existing rules and regulations
governing the utilization of the said fund.

We recommended that Management and the LSB to:

a. Ensure that the prescribed planning and budgeting tools are effectively
utilized in order to prepare an optimal and timely SEF Budget; and
b. Strictly conform to the timelines in the planning and budgeting process as
prescribed under DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circular No. 2017-01.

We also recommended that the previous years’ unexpended balances be indicated
in the Income and Expenditures Estimates for the budget year so that the total amount
available for appropriation could be determined to ensure funding of PPAs of public
schools in the municipality.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the:

a) Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over the remittance
of Inter-Agency Payables through strict coordination with the personnel in-
charge of payroll, reconciliation between the billing statement and the payroll
deductions, and ensure that remittances are based on actual collections and
payroll deductions;
b) Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere and observe deadlines for remittances
to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth in order to avoid penalties/surcharges; and
c) Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation and trace the cause of the
abnormal negative balances of these accounts and effect the necessary
adjusting entries.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the:

a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the records of
their offices to determine the Accountable/Liquidating Officers who have
outstanding undeposited collections, trace collections already deposited but
not recorded in the books, and to verify the actual accountability of the
Municipal Treasurer;
b. Municipal Treasurer to ensure that collections from various collecting
officers are remitted daily to the Liquidating Officer/s and the same are
regularly deposited to the Authorized Government Depository Bank
(AGDB). The Liquidating Officer must verify the unused accountable forms
in the possession of collecting clerks upon remittance to see to it that all
collections are remitted daily and intact. The new booklet of accountable
forms must only be issued upon full consumption of the whole stub to prevent
the collecting clerks from retaining huge amount of collections in their
custody; and
c. Municipal Accountant to record the undeposited collections as accountability
of the actual persons responsible and to ensure that deposits thereof are
recorded in the books.

We recommended that Management require the:

a) Budget/Accounting Office to prepare and submit to the NDRRMC, through
the OCD, and to the Office of the Auditor the report on the receipt and
utilization of future cash donations; and
b) Municipal Accountant to monitor the transactions in the trust fund books most
especially fund transfers and diligently apply relevant accounting treatment
thereto.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC to require the
aforementioned suppliers to prepare or submit the Mayor’s/Business Permit, OSS and
the Income/Business Tax Returns. After these documents were verified and examined,
the same must be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for the required validation to
avoid audit suspension of the involved transactions.

observations.managementAction: Implemented
Management promised to comply with the recommendations.
observations.reason: The establishment of RPT and SET Receivable at the beginning of the year was not prioritize. Although, the Assessor’s Office provided the Annual Assessment for CY 2019, no certified list of taxpayers was provided by the Municipal Treasurer to the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: 7. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant promised to conduct a detailed review and will provide necessary adjustments to correct the balances.
observations.reason: Review of these reciprocal accounts was not made possible due to the bulk of transactions processed in the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation:
8. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by requiring the Municipal Accountant to review thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies during the month and that remittances must be based on the actual payroll deductions. 

9. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyze every transaction before recording in the books. Assign personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office promised review the schedules and to establish additional controls to prevent this deficiency from happening again.
observations.reason: Remittances were in excess of the monthly collection or payroll deductions. Review of these accounts was not made.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

10. Create a General Services Officer (GSO) position that will discharge on a full time basis the property custodianship functions and the receipt, issuance and recording of the procurement and issuance of supplies and property, maintain updated Property Cards per category on all property, plant and equipment as recorded in the list of Property, Plant and Equipment of accounting records and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the RPCPPE;

11. Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain property ledger cards consistent with their accounting records; and

12. Maintain and renew all Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment every three years for the signature of the holder of each item listed in the prepared RPCPPE or based on the old Memorandum Receipts on file.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to prioritize the conduct of inventory-taking and the preparation and submission of the required RPCPPE.
observations.reason: The creation of a GSO was not considered in CY 2019 because of budget issues. The Municipal Treasurer explained that the conduct of inventory-taking was not possible due to time constraint. Maintenance of PPELC was not prioritize by Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: 13. We recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation based on available documents especially for those recently acquired properties and make the necessary adjustment on the affected accounts. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the depreciable properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant promised to review the documents of the previous years to determine the data needed for the provision of depreciation to some of the PPEs.
observations.reason: Provision of Depreciation to some PPEs were still not prioritize. Some PPEs were purchased in previous years and lack sufficient documents that will show their description, number of units, acquisition cost and date of acquisition.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

14. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 51 of the MNGAS for LGUs on the proper recording and reporting of transactions which requires that all regular purchases of supplies must be booked through the inventory account whether it is consumed or not within the accounting period and maintenance of  subsidiary ledger cards;

15. Direct each Department Head to conduct an inventory to establish the existence of the office supplies, medicines and other items/inventories for stock to serve as basis for reconciliation of the same with the reports of the accounting; and

16. Create a General Services Officer (GSO) position, if warranted, that will discharge on a full time basis the property custodianship functions and the receipt, issuance and recording of the procurement and issuance of supplies and property; maintain stock cards per category on all inventories as recorded in the list of Inventory accounting records; and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI).
observations.reason: Accounting Office recorded the purchases of inventories directly as expense. The creation of GSO was not considered in CY 2019 because of budget issues.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality:

27. Revisit the provisions of the CY 2009 Revenue Code with the end-in-view of considering the call of the present times towards the best interest of the public and the government; and

28. Update the existing tax rates, fees and charges prescribed in the Revenue Code to be able to sustain an adequate, appropriate and relevant tax rates in relation to the Municipality’s fast-paced growth and development requirements.
observations.reason: The Municipality still uses the 2008 Municipal Revenue Code. The revision of it was not prioritize by Management in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
	
38. That the Office of the Municipal Accountant give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the required bank reconciliation statements pursuant to Section 474 of RA No. 7160 and COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

39. That the cash books of the Municipal Treasurer be updated always, and the data reflected in these books be considered in the preparation of the bank reconciliation statements to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted;

40. Any error and reconciling item discovered in the reconciliation process be adjusted in the respective records which need correction/revision, to arrive at correct and reliable cash in bank balance.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will organize her department and will assign a personnel that will be responsible for the preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements.
observations.reason: Due to lack of manpower and considering the bank reconciliation is a very time consuming task, they cannot give priority on its preparation.

The maintenance of Property Cards for the PPE of the Municipality was not given importance and priority by the Inventory Custodian and Municipal Accountant. Nonetheless, the Municipality already appointed a Supply Officer to facilitate the PPE and Inventory management of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: 45. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant compute the depreciation based on available documents especially for those recently acquired properties and make the necessary adjustment on the affected accounts. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will gather documents that will be the bases for computation of the PPEs depreciation.
observations.reason: The provision of depreciation for some of the Municipality’s PPE was not prioritized by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation:
46. We recommended that Management strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by requiring the Municipal Accountant to review thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies every month and that remittances must be based on the actual collection vis-à-vis payroll deductions. 

47. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyze transactions before recording in the books and update entries of both records. Further, assign personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant did not conduct detailed review of the previous records of the stated accounts.

observations.recommendation: 52. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert extra effort to prepare updated bank reconciliation statements for all funds and to submit the same to COA for verification. It will be used as basis to ascertain and validate the balances recorded in the Financial Statements for CY 2016.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to comply with the herein stated audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Due to lack of manpower and considering the bank reconciliation is a very time consuming task, they cannot give priority on its preparation.

observations.recommendation: 60. We reiterated compliance to the audit recommendations offered in the previous audit reports which primarily dealt on the assurance of conducting physical count of LGU properties and submitting updated RPCPPE to the auditor concerned.
observations.managementAction: The Property Custodian still promised to exert more efforts to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: The Property Custodian, who is also the Municipal Treasurer, cannot give priority to the property custodianship, issuance and inventory-taking, due to the demanding nature of her responsibility as Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the required BRS, pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011, and prepare subsidiary ledgers per bank accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously the cash books and the data reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of the BRS to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require that an actual inventory-taking of all assets or PPEs of the LGU be conducted each year pursuant to Section 124, Volume I of NGAS Manual for LGUs, that the relevant RPCPPE be submitted not later than January 31 of each year to the Office of the Auditor and that the same be reconciled with the records of the Accountant.
We also recommended that Management consider creating a General Services Office (GSO) that will discharge on a full time basis the procurement and issuance of supplies and property; maintain updated Property Cards per category on all Agency’s assets as recorded in the list of PPE of accounting records; and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation expense of the PPE with available documents, using the method prescribed in the aforementioned PAGs. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prioritize the provision of depreciation for the assets procured/constructed from year 2015 onwards, review PPE schedules, gather supporting documents attached on the disbursement vouchers related to the PPE and make necessary adjustments on the affected accounts. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by reviewing thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies during each month and ensure that remittances are based on the actual payroll deductions. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyze every transactions before recording them in the books and assign a personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Municipal Accountant, the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (LDRRMO), the Budget Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to review all available documents relative to LDRRMF and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the financial statements and other documents;
Instruct the aforementioned officers to revisit and strictly follow the provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 10121, COA Circular No. 2012-002 and National Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council – Department of Budget and Management – Department of Interior and Local Government (NDRRMC-DBM-DILG) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for transfers of Agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the Special TF by year of transfer, receipts of NDRRMF by transferring agency, DRRMF from other LGUs by LGU and other sources by donor.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and all others who are involved in the preparation of the financial statements and their supporting schedules, immediately submit the required financial reports.
Strict compliance with the rules and regulations on the submission of monthly and quarterly financial reports to the Auditor within the prescribed period as required under Section 70 of NGAS Manual for LGUs and Section 122 of PD 1445 is enjoined.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require all AOs to submit necessary documents to support their claims to the Office of the Municipal Accountant and direct the Municipal Accountant to submit all the disbursement vouchers together with their supporting documents from January to December 2019 to the Office of the Auditor;
Evaluate the process of preparing the disbursement vouchers and necessary documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation; and
Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected copies of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review.
We also directed the attention of Management to Section 4.0, the Penalty Clause, of COA Circular No. 2009-001, which states that the inability of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements of the same must be subject to administrative disciplinary action and the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents be may be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the LDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to secure immediately the needed documents to facilitate the timely preparation of the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and submit a copy thereof to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, pursuant to the procedures of the aforecited Circular, to facilitate verification thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the LDRRMO II and Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO) to initiate the creation and filling up of the mandatory positions prescribed under the NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC 2014-1 to properly institutionalize the organization of the LDRRM Office and to have continuity in service and implementation of its programs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the LCPC to actively perform their duties and responsibilities to ensure full strengthening and implementation of the LCPC PPAs and widen the scope of the PPAs for LCPC as enumerated in Section II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 and prioritize the PPAs with long term effects/involvement on children.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the SWM Focal Person to prioritize the approval of the MSWMP that will ensure the efficient management of solid wastes generated within the Municipality’s jurisdiction; and re-organize the MSWMB, compliant with prescribed composition enumerated in Section 16 of RA No. 9003, that will adapt measures to promote the effective implementation of the solid waste management programs.

We recommended that Management implement immediately the PPAs pertinent to the unutilized balance of ₱8.03 million as embodied in the Annual Investment Program (AIP). We also recommended that Management judiciously plan the PPAs to be funded out of the 20 per cent LDF and ensure its full utilization for the benefit of the constituents and local government itself, in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We have recommended that the Accounting Division verify the journal entries made relative to the Nego Cart, concreting of Farm to Market Road in various barangays, Construction of Water Pumping Station for Farming located in different barangays as well as the Construction of Multi-Purpose Drying Pavement and to make the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts for fair presentation of the Other Property, Plant and Equipment, Road Networks, Water Supply Systems and Other Infrastructure Project accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The necessary correcting/ adjusting entries were already effected in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We therefore reiterate our previous year’s recommendation that management strictly comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations pertaining to granting and liquidation of cash advances. It is further recommended that: 

1.	No additional Cash Advance shall be given to officials/employees unless the previous cash advances was first settled;

2.	All cash advances shall be supported by an Accountant’s Certification that the requesting party has no existing unliquidated cash advances; and

3.	Withhold the salaries of officials and employees whose cash advance remains unliquidated once efforts yield no encouraging results.
observations.managementAction: The cash advance amounting to ₱254,975.00 already liquidated.
observations.reason: The liquidation papers were already post-audited and the corresponding entries were taken up in the books.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the procedures in the establishment replenishment cash advance granted under Petty Cash Fund in accordance with Section 4.3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 48 of Vol. I of MNGAS.
observations.managementAction: The audit recommendation was already complied by adopting the imprest system in the maintenance of Petty Cash Fund.
observations.reason: Expenses were recognized in the replenishment of the PCF.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant and ICO-Municipal Treasurer ensure that provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the DSWD are adhered to, which include compliance on proper and complete documentation, before effecting the payment.  Also, we also have recommended that management refrain from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit. The LGU should also formulate concrete basis/guidelines for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.

It is also  recommended that a separate Petty Cash Fund with a reasonable amount be set up to facilitate the payment of financial assistance for burial, transportation, medical assistance and other emergency needs with an amount not exceeding ₱3,000.00. Pursuant to the general policies of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008, these shall be paid in outright cash directly to the client. It will also eliminate numerous reimbursements of financial assistance.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant and ICO-Municipal Treasurer were already guided by provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the DSWD in granting financial assistance to indigents. Also, managed has already refrained from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes.
observations.reason: Compliance of the LGU was validated in the post-audit of transaction.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as Chairman of the Local School Board, strictly adhere with the rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of the SEF particularly for the construction and repair of school buildings and establishment of new extension classes or acquisition of books and instructional materials of students and teachers in public schools pursuant to DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 and 1A in order to ensure an effective educational development within the locality.  

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims are duly supported with complete documentation in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445 before effecting payments to avoid issuance of notice of suspension/disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The utilization of Special Education Fund were guided by  Sections 100 (c) and 272 of Republic Act No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular Nos. 1  series of 2017.
observations.reason: Compliance of the LGU was validated in the post-audit of transaction.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its SEF in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.
observations.managementAction: The Local School Board has already included allocations from its SEF in support of the ECCD Program.
observations.reason: It was validated in the school board budget.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to determine the cause of the negative balance of the Due from LGUs account and make the necessary adjustment in the books.  We also recommended that the OIC- Municipal Accountant ensure that transactions relative to the receivable account do not exceed the amount of receivables taken up in the books in order avoid the recurrence of negative balance of account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Necessary adjustments were already effected in the books.
observations.reason: The related Journal Entry Voucher was verified.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Accounting Office to verify the journal entries to determine the correct adjusting journal entry in the books to reconcile reciprocal accounts.
observations.managementAction: Necessary adjustments were already effected in the books.
observations.reason: The related Journal Entry Voucher was verified.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all other concern strictly adhere to the policies/guidelines governing the hiring of Job Orders (JOs) and their entitlements. Management should stop from approving travel order of Job Orders to attend conference/seminars to prevent the LGU’s coffers being used to activities in which it could not benefit.  

We further recommended the refund of the amount reimbursed to the JOs in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2019, no JO was allowed to attend training and seminars.
observations.reason: In the post-audit of transaction for CY 2019, there was no charge of seminar or training expenses by the JOs, but the amount reimbursed to the JOs in 2018 not yet refunded.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Office of the Auditor be notified by ICO- Municipal Treasurer as “the official responsible for or in-charge of accepting deliveries of procured items” pursuant to No. 7.4 and No 7.7 of COA Circular No. 94-006 and Training Handbook on Property & Supply Management System to enable the COA Audit Team to conduct timely inspection to ascertain the correctness of quantity and propriety of specification and purchase description of items delivered.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Treasurer has a written notification on the delivery of goods.
observations.reason: COA received Notice of Delivery from the Treasury Office.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretary and OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents enumerated therein in order to complete the contract review and technical evaluation said project to avoid issuance of notice of suspension.
observations.managementAction: The LGU has complied to settle the deficiencies noted in the audit of procurement of infrastructure, goods and services.
observations.reason: Compliance of the LGU was validated in the post-audit of transaction.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management requires the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of trial balance and bank reconciliation statements on the 20th day of the following month and submission of interim reports on the 20th day after the end of each quarter pursuant to Sections 70 and 73, Volume 1 of MNGAS and ensure that the necessary attachment to the trial balance be also submitted in compliance with the above-cited provision.
observations.managementAction: Trial Balance and Bank Reconciliation Statements were already submitted on time.
observations.reason: Submissions of said reports were monitored.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and ICO-Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱ 1,611,830.70 under the General Fund to the Special Turst Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Circular and revert back the unexpended balance of Trust Liabilities –DRRM for CY 2012 from Trust Fund to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund which could be used for other social services through enactment by the Local Sanggunian of the Municipality.

Verified the transfer in the Bank Statement and Journal Entry Voucher in May 2019:
Under JEV# 300-019-05-DV069  (2012); and
JEV# 300-019-05-DV070 (2013).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

1. Advise the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions of the New Government Accounting System Manual for Local Government Units (NGAS-LGUs), COA Circular 2010-001 and IPSAS I in the submission of the complete set of financial reports on a monthly, quarterly and annual basis; and

2. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over the record keeping of the Municipality’s transactions and to devise and implement strategies that will help the Accounting Office in the performance of its duties and responsibilities.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to implement more controls to strengthen accounting processes and will consider additional personnel in the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: The Accounting Office evaluated their process and implemented additional accounting controls to improve their processes, however, CY 2019 financial statement were still submitted beyond the prescribed period. The Municipal Accountant explained that this is due to unforeseen events, such as equipment malfunction.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
 
17. Direct the officials and employees of the Municipality to submit promptly their claims or reports to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for review, verification and recording purposes;

18. Require the Municipal Accountant to fast track the submission of the said accounts complete with their respective supporting documents and to evaluate their current system and existing manpower for any necessary improvement or augmentation to prevent the further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding financial years; and

19. Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to conduct meeting with the concerned employees.
observations.reason: Although the CY 2018 disbursement accounts were submitted to the Office of the Auditor, the disbursement vouchers for CY 2019 transactions were submitted beyond the prescribed period and some were still not submitted.

observations.recommendation: 20. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected contracts and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to conduct meeting with the concerned employees.
observations.reason: The MPDO submitted the copies of some of the contracts with incomplete documentation as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-001.

observations.recommendation:
21. We recommended that the Municipality prioritize the corrections of the 10-year SWM Plan and submit it to the EMB Regional Office V and NSWC for approval, as required under Section 16 of RA 9003 to ensure the efficient management of solid waste generated within its jurisdiction.

22. We recommended that the Municipality comply with the provisions of RA 9003 and DENR AO No. 9 and issue an ordinance for the closure and rehabilitation of the Municipality’s existing controlled dumpsite located at Brgy. Burgos, San Jacinto, Masbate.

23. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor evaluate the composition of the Municipality Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) and look for possible members that could qualify to complete the lineup.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to exert more efforts to fast-track the approval of the MSWMP, so as to implement the programs and projects incorporated on it.
observations.reason: The MSWMP was submitted again to the EMB-ROV for checking, however, no response was received yet as to the matter. The Municipality purchased a lot that will be used as the sanitary landfill.

observations.recommendation: 24. We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare monthly the Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and instruct the LDRRM Officer to submit a copy thereof to the office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, pursuant to the procedures of the forecited Circular, to facilitate verification thereof.
observations.managementAction: LDRRMO will verify his records with the records of the Budget and Accounting Office and promised to submit the required report monthly.
observations.reason: The LDRRMO submitted not monthly but quarterly report of utilization. However, upon checking this still needs verification with the records of the Accounting and the Budget Offices.

observations.recommendation:
25. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasury Office to implement collection strategies to further improve revenue assessment and collection. 
26. We also recommended that the Sanguniang Bayan devise an ordinance that will assist the Treasury Office in assessing the basis for CTC tax, such as requiring the CTC taxpayers to file a sworn declaration of total earnings derived from business, compensation, exercise of profession and income from real property during the preceding year.
observations.reason: Although the Municipality implemented strategies to increase the collection of CTC, it was still not enough to reach the projected collection of CTC per previous AOM.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

29. Direct the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children to actively perform their duties and responsibilities to ensure full strengthening and implementation of the LCPC programs, projects and activities; 

30. Widen the scope of the programs, projects and activities for LCPC as enumerated in Section II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 and prioritize the PPAs with long term effects/involvement on children; and

31. Institute strong internal control over municipal transactions to ensure that the municipal government has a balanced budget and that funds allocated for the delivery of basic services and development projects are utilized as intended.

We recommended that Management:

32. Judiciously plan the programs/projects/activities to be funded out of the 20 per cent EDF in accordance with the mandate of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 2, 2017; and 

33. Promptly implement all the programs/projects/activities as embodied in the approved Annual Development Plan in order to optimize the delivery of basic services to the people.
observations.reason: Some were still not implemented by the Agency.

observations.managementAction: The Management will send demand letters and enforce settlement of the undeposited collections.
observations.reason: The Management started settlement of this account. The collectors remit collections to the Municipal Treasurer. Maintenance of subsidiary ledgers for accountable officers was not prioritize by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: 48. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal officials and employees concerned to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected contracts and their supporting documents to COA for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review, the results of which will be used in post-audit of disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The BAC will submit the contracts of all PPAs of the Municipality together with their complete supporting documents enumerated in Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.
observations.reason: The BAC Chairman submits contracts of some PPAs with incomplete supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: 49. We recommended to require the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to provide the Office of the Auditor at the beginning of the year a list of all on-going and to be implemented PPAs during the year. Further, a monthly monitoring report on the status of the projects must be submitted. Likewise, the Municipal Engineer must see to it that verification of the projects’ status as reported in its monthly report in accordance with the frequency of verification provided in Item 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 be timely requested and the necessary documents be submitted to facilitate the inspection by COA-TAS.

50. We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare monthly the Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and instruct the LDRRM Officer to submit a copy thereof to the office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, pursuant to the procedures of the forecited Circular, to facilitate verification thereof.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will prepare the Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and the LDRRM Officer will submit the said report to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: The LDRRM Officer prepares only quarterly report on the utilization of the DRRMF, and this is subject to reconciliation with the records of the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: 51. We recommended that the LGU prioritize the preparation and submission of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan to the National Solid Waste Management Commission for approval, as required under Section 16 of RA 9003 to ensure the efficient management of solid waste generated within its jurisdiction. We also recommended the Municipality to immediately close the LGUs existing controlled dumpsite and construct a sanitary landfill.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality will exert more efforts to fast track the approval of their SWM Plan by the NSWC; and will devise an ordinance for the closure of the controlled dumpsite.
observations.reason: The Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality is on its second revision and to be submitted again to the EMB Region V for correction/validation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

53. Strictly enforce the settlement of all unliquidated cash advances as of December 30,2016; 

54. Stop the granting of additional cash advances to Accountable Officers, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled, to avoid accumulation of the same; and 

55. Institute the appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those erring accountable officers/employees for continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Management promised to exert more efforts in the liquidation of various cash advances.
observations.reason: The Management explained that they already sent a demand letters to the Accountable Officers transferred to other LGU but AOs refused to receive them. Nonetheless, the Management promised to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
56. We recommended that the Head of the Agency require the Solid Waste Management Board, the Municipal Planning Office and all the Departments involve in planning and conceptualizing the Solid Waste Management Plan to give their full attention and cooperation in the revision and finalization of the draft Solid Waste Management Plan of the LGU to be able to comply with the requirement of the National Solid Waste Management Commission.

57. We likewise recommended that the Local Chief Executive appoint a permanent employee who will be in-charge of the operation of the Solid Waste Management Board.
observations.managementAction: The Management will exert more efforts for the approval of the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management Plan.
observations.reason: The Municipality established a Solid Waste Management Board, however the Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality is still under second revision.

observations.recommendation:
58. We recommended requiring the Revenue Collection Officers to strictly turnover all their collections intact and in full amount at the close of the business day to the Municipal Treasurer. Likewise, the collections turned over to the Municipal Treasurer including her collections has to be deposited intact to the bank daily or not later than the next banking day. She must likewise closely monitor the turn-over/remittances of collecting clerks to prevent possible misuse/misapplication of cash.
	
59. We also recommended that Management immediately prepare and send demand letter to the previous and current accountable officers requiring them to fully settle the balance of their accountability.
observations.managementAction: The Accountable Officers turnovers all their collections to the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: Due to the inability of the Management to demand settlement of the undeposited collections of the previous accountable officers.

observations.recommendation: 61. We recommended that Management require the General Services Officer to submit copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders and their complete supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) days after their perfection for purposes of review of said contracts and technical evaluation by COA TAS.
observations.managementAction: The Management promised to submit copy of perfected contracts together with the supporting documents within five (5) working days from the execution of contract by the government.
observations.reason:
Due to lack of coordination between the BAC and Accounting Department.

The BAC submits only the Contract Agreement, Notice of Award, Notice to Proceed and BAC Resolution of some of the PPAs of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
Facilitate the creation on an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of  properties of the municipality. The actual count could be undertaken any month during the current  year, but it has to be updated at year-end. A copy of the Inventory Report has to be submitted to COA not later than January 31of each year.
Reconcile the PPE per inventory report with accounting records/reports. Any discrepancies shall b verified and adjusted accordingly.  Recipient of any missing PPEs be held accountable.
Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.
Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.
Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.
Make use of Invenory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper accountability and control.
During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal.
Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.
Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. Moreover, create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

We have reiterated our audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Acting Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to prepare and furnish the OIC-Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting-up  receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of  accounts in the financial statements.
We have also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to determine the cause/s of the negative balances of the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax accounts and make the necessary adjustment in the books.  Further, we have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant ensure that transactions relative to the RPT/SET receivables and Deferred RPT/SET accounts shall not have negative account balances at a given period.

We have recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to record purchases of supplies and materials by using the perpetual inventory method except for purchases out of the petty cash fund which is to be recorded as an outright expense. Issuance of supplies and materials shall be supported by a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which will be consolidated in a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI). It shall be the basis of the Accounting Unit to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize the monthly issuance of supplies and materials in the books of accounts.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive require periodic physical inventory of supplies or property.  Physical count of inventory items by type shall be conducted semestrally and shall be reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). This shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
