We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain individual subsidiary ledgers for all accounts as required under Sections 111 and 114 PD No. 1445 and to exert diligent efforts to locate the necessary supporting documents to determine the exact age of the receivable account and to identify the government agencies and individuals/debtors or entities and collect the said obligations/receivables from them.
When these accounts are confirmed to be non-moving for more than five (5) years which are considered to be dormant accounts, and no available records or documents on file, the Management can request for write-off of the dormant accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 for fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Assessor to exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership of the lots through a Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in the name of the Municipal Government of San Jacinto in compliance with the provisions of Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual in order to protect the interest of the Municipality for any adverse claims by third parties.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to transfer 100% of the local road account from the Registry of Public Infrastructure to the General Fund which will form part of the Property, Plant and Equipment under Road Networks account pursuant to the provision of COA Circular No. 2015-008.  We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers for the Road Networks account to facilitate reconciliation of inventory report of said account with accounting records.

We therefore reiterate the previous year’s audit recommendation that management strictly comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations pertaining to granting and liquidation of cash advances. We also recommended the Local Chief Executive to ensure that:
No additional Cash Advance shall be given to officials/employees unless the previous cash advance was first settled;
All cash advances shall be supported by an Accountant’s Certification that the requesting party has no existing unliquidated cash advances; and
Cash advances of the municipal officials and employees amounting to ₱68,100.00 be liquidated; otherwise, withhold the salaries of officials and employees whose cash advances remain unliquidated once efforts yield no encouraging results.

We have recommended that Management conduct physical inventory of all Property, Plant and Equipment to determine the actual insurable properties of the LGU.  We also recommended that Management submit updated inventory report of properties to the GSIS for appraisal purposes to determine the updated amount of premiums for the insurance of all insurable assets, and allocate additional budget for the payment of insurance premiums, if necessary.

Management commented that the foregoing audit recommendations shall be complied with. The Audit Team was informed that the Online Payroll System shall be adopted by the LGU to prevent possible risk robbery hold-up and other similar incidents

Management commented that the audit recommendation shall be complied with. The Sangguniang Bayan Members shall be inform to stop the practice of granting financial assistance/donations to the constituents of San Jacinto.  Management agreed to direct the appropriated funds (for financial assistance) to the MSWDO of the Municipality. Also, the Accounting Office shall be reminded of their duty to record the financial assistance granted to individuals under Donation account for proper presentation of account in the financial statements.

To strengthen the internal control in the use of government vehicles and to regulate fuel consumption of the Municipality, we have recommended Management the following courses of action:
For motor vehicle which is not exclusively use by any government official, to require the return of the vehicle in the office to have it full tank each time the vehicle is used, thus the actual no. of  liters consumed for each travel is immediately determined and the same shall be indicated in the Driver’s Trip Ticket;
To ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes and maintain a separate file copy of the Driver’s Trip Ticket; and
Ensure that dates of travel per Driver’s Trip Ticket are consistent with the dates indicated in the Travel Order and Certificate of Appearance.
We also recommended that policies and guidelines as clearly defined under COA Circular 77-61 be given due regard for proper control and monitoring of the use of government vehicle and efficient fuel consumption.
Further, we have recommended that the SB Officials concerned refund the amount claimed for gasoline consumption, to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.
There was no comment of management on the foregoing audit observations and recommendations.

Management commented that the honoraria were charged against the appropriation for General Fund instead of the proceeds from the sale of bids documents, pointing out the provision of the DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-003 dated November 29, 2007 which amended DBM Circular No. 2004-5A dated October 7, 2005 where it expressly allows the sourcing of the BAC Honoraria from the General Fund through Savings.
Furthermore, the BAC honorarium was not charged from the available proceeds of the sales of bid documents etc., amounting to ₱495,401.94 because of the following reasons:
The source of fund for the payment of honoraria is not only limited to the collection from the proceeds of the sales of bid documents, etc. which is under the Trust Fund account but it could be sourced out from the savings of the LGU as it was provided under DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-003 dated November 29, 2007.
The amount of ₱495,401.94 was not enough to cover the Municipality’s BAC honoraria;
3) The BAC Committee deemed it best that the said amount would be used instead for the day-to-day administrative and operational expenses of the Committee in the performance of their duties.
With regard to the incomplete documentation, management signified to submit the required documents and also instructed the OIC- Municipal Accountant to deduct the corresponding withholding taxes in all disbursement of BAC honorarium pursuant to BIR rules and regulations.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
While the provisions of DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-003 provides the funding source for the payment of BAC honoraria which included savings from local budgets, the Audit Team maintain our stand on the audit observation and recommendation on the basis that collections derived from proceeds on the sale of bid documents and other   procurement-related activities, as mentioned in 3.1.1 of the foregoing budget circular, shall be the primary source of the payment of honoraria. That in cases of deficiency in collections from procurement activities, savings of the LGU shall be considered as additional funding source, subject to conditions provided in the same circular.  It was provided in 3.4 of Budget Circular 2007-3 that the manner of payment of honoraria and overtime pay charged against collections enumerated in 3.1.1 and savings cited under 3.2 of said circular shall still be in accordance with item no. 6.2 of Budget Circular No. 2004-5A.
As it was given emphasis by management that savings “arising from unpaid compensation…” could be used for the payment of honoraria and overtime pay for procurement activities,  there were instances when the President of the Philippines will grant additional incentives at the end of the year where the funding source is largely depended on savings.  Item 3.3 of the same circular provided that “In the use of savings, priority shall be given to augmentation of the amount set aside for mandatory expenditure items provided under the General Provisions of the GAA. It is understood that the use of agency savings for the payment of honoraria and overtime pay for procurement activities shall be made only after satisfying said mandatory expenditure items.”  (underscoring supplied)

Management commented that the foregoing audit recommendation shall be complied with. Management assured the Audit Team that they will be more cautious in complying with the rates and schedule prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-003A. The Human Resource Management Officer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant will also be more thorough in their submission of the necessary documentary requirements prescribed under Section 5.15 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the grant of Loyalty Cash Award to qualified LGU officials or employees.

We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant strictly use the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Personnel Officer/HRMO and OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the Daily Time Records (DTRs) for CY 2019 of all Public Health Workers and other personnel concerned to support the validity of the claims on hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances.
We also recommended that Management strictly observe the rules and regulations in granting hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances and ensure that all claims pertaining thereto have to be supported with the required Daily Time Records and other required documents pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 2012, DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1, S, 2016 and DBM-DSWD Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2018.

We have recommended the creation of Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and designation of a focal person to carry out effectively the implementation of programs, projects and activities related to Solid Waste Management that is geared towards the preservation and protection of the environment and public health as required under R.A. No.  9003.  We also recommended that management involve the participation of the barangay officials in information dissemination on the importance of segregations of waste at source, and to forewarn them that incineration of solid waste  be avoided because it is prohibited by law.

We have recommended that the management ensure that the projects/programs/activities designed under the 20% Development Fund are implemented fully within the time frame. Optimal utilization of the 20% IRA is also recommended in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We have recommended that Management require the current Municipal School Board (MSB) to reorganize the composition of the School Board and include the SK Federation President, the Teacher’s Organization in the Municipality and the duly elected representative of the Non-Academic Personnel of Public Schools in the Municipality for proper representation therein.  Further, cause the removal of the other two members who are not included as qualified members pursuant to the provisions of Section 98(3) of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 7160.

Management commented that they will instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting or correcting entries to record the 15% share of the Office of the Building Official from collections of Building Permit fees and transfer the same as Trust Liabilities under Trust Fund.

We have recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its SEF in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.
There was no comment of management on the foregoing audit observations and recommendations.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to transfer the PCSO funds received from Trust Fund to the General Fund and ensure that appropriations are provided therefor in the utilization thereof;
Submit copies of Memorandum of Agreement entered into between the LGU with PCSO for CY 2017, CY 2018 and CY 2019 indicating the category of programs and projects including the amount of allocation or funds and the reportorial requirements pertaining to its utilization as stated in Section 40 of the 2016 Revised IRR for the Small Town Lottery Operations of the PCSO.
Management commented that the OIC-Municipal Accountant shall be instructed to make the necessary transfer of the PCSO funds from Trust Fund to General Fund and ensure that these funds are properly appropriated.

Management commented that they will immediately stop the granting of honorarium to the President of FESCAP in compliance with Section 3, Article 21, Rule VI of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No.9994, also known as “The Expanded Senior Citizens Act of 2010.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive strictly adhere to the policies/guidelines governing the hiring of Job Orders (JOs) and their entitlements. We also recommended that Management should stop from granting Overtime Pay to Job Order personnel considering that they are not included in the list of government employees who may be authorized to render overtime with pay or compensation pursuant to Section 6.1 of CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2015 dated November 25, 2015.
We further recommended the refund of the amount reimbursed to the JOs in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.
Management commented that that the audit recommendations will be strictly observe. The policies/guidelines governing the hiring of Job Orders (JOs) and their entitlements, as well as the stoppage of the granting of Overtime Pay to Job Order Personnel.

Management commented that audit recommendations shall be complied with. Management is willing to create a committee to undertake a thorough study and evaluation on the actual workloads and manpower requirements of the Municipality for each Department/Office. Further, submission of accomplishment reports shall be required from Job Order Personnel as basis for payment.  The Human Resource Officer shall also comply to submit copies of contract of services of JOs for review purposes and to support the legality of said disbursements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to process only those disbursement vouchers with complete documentation pursuant to the provision of Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.  We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant should submit all the above-mentioned supporting documents to the audit team to avoid suspension/ disallowance in audit.
Management commented that they instructed the OIC-Municipal Accountant to process vouchers with complete documentation and submit the same to COA to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that electronic or computerized system in assessment and collection be considered and adopted by the LGU of San Jacinto which is a time and cost efficient scheme in processing transactions. It will also facilitate the preparation of reports which are accurate and reliable. Furthermore, it will prevent/minimize errors and inconsistencies in the assessment and collection of taxes and other revenues of the Municipality, whether intentional or not, thus, it will improve or increase the collection of the LGU. Considering the benefits it could give to management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby encourage.

We have recommended that Management stop the practice of granting Transportation Allowance (TA) to officials who are assigned and presently using government vehicles pursuant to Section 8.2.1 of Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.

We have recommended that Management strictly observe the provision of Section 7.1 of Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013 in granting RATA to municipal officials to ensure that payment thereof is duly supported with the required documents which shall be the basis in determining the amount of RATA to be granted for each period.  We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer should ensure that claims for RATA are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment thereof.
Further, we have recommended that Management require the refund of the RATA granted to the Municipal Civil Registrar and Human Resource Management Officer in the amount of ₱18,000.00 and ₱3,000.00, respectively, pursuant to Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that claims of Representation and Transportation Allowance be supported with the required documents which be the basis in determining whether the provisions of the afore cited laws, rules and regulations were observed in granting RATA. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure that claims for RATA are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment thereof.
Management commented that the foregoing audit recommendations shall be complied with.  Management ensured that supporting documents shall be attached to support the payment of Representation and Transportation Allowance to concerned Municipal Employees.

We have recommended that management release the shares of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter pursuant to Section 271 of Republic Act 7160 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for development projects which could be beneficial to their constituents.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive advice the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to strictly comply with Section 7 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184 and Updated Budget Operations Manual for Local Government Units in the preparation of APP and PPMP.  The BAC must carefully plan and schedule the agency’s procurement in order to manage its resources, produce economical result, and make the procurement procedures more efficient and practical.
We also recommended that Management ensure that the prescribed format under DBM Circular Letter 2011-6 be strictly followed in the preparation of APP-CSE  to come up with a realistic budget in the procurement of common use supplies and equipment.

We have recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and approved by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
Management commented that the audit recommendation shall be complied with. Procurement Monitoring Report shall be prepared to be approved by the HOPE and be submitted to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all department heads of the LGU to purchase common-use supplies and equipment at the Procurement Service pursuant to Administrative Order No. 17 s.  2011 and DBM Circular Letter No, 2011-6 to avail the most advantageous price for the government. When goods are to be purchased from local/private suppliers, ensure that these shall always be supported by an evidence of non-availability of stocks in the PS and shall be purchased at reasonable prices.
Management commented that  the LGU had resorted to local and private suppliers in the purchase of some common-use supplies due to the fact that DBM-Procurement Service does not usually have a complete inventory of the items listed in the LGUs Agency Procurement Plan (APR). Management justified that it was not their intention to defy Administrative Order No. 17 s. 2011 and DBM Circular Letter No. 2011-6. Nonetheless, Management will comply with the audit recommendation, however, in case when the requested common-use supplies were not available in DBM-PS, resorting to local and private suppliers shall not be avoided so as not to disrupt the operation of the LGU.

We have recommended the following to address the foregoing issues, to wit:
a. That the Acting Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the  above-mentioned provisions whereby all collections shall be deposited intact and daily or not later than the next banking day with the authorized depository bank to avoid possible loss or misuse of government funds.
That the Acting Municipal Treasurer should ensure that all checks to be released to the payees/claimants must be properly acknowledged by affixing their signatures in the Check Register.
That the Acting Municipal Treasurer notifies the payees on the existence of the stale checks.  We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts on the stale checks and the cost of documentary stamps in order to reflect the correct amount of the Cash in Bank and other related accounts in the financial statements.
The pro-forma accounting entry for Lost/Destroyed/Stale/Obsolete Checks is as follows:
Checks issued in the current/prior year for replacement:
Check cancellation           Cash in Bank-LCCA       xxx
Accounts Payable                xxx
Replacement		       Accounts Payable          xxx
Cash in Bank – LCCA         xxx
Management commented that the Acting Municipal Treasurer shall be instructed to notify the payees on the existence of stale check. Likewise, the OIC-Municipal Accountant shall also be instructed to effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts.
Management commented that a person responsible in certifying loan applications shall be instructed to take into consideration the net take home pay of concerned applicants.

Management commented that from the time the issue was first raised in CY 2017, a total of eight (8) vacant positions were already filled up as of December 31, 2019.  five (5) in CY 2018 and three (3) in CY 2019. The Audit Team was informed that additional five (5) positions were already filled up for the month of  January 2020.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
While management has already filled up eight (8) vacant positions, the three (3) key positions which consisted of department head positions  were not yet filled up to date, thus funds allocated for such positions, which comprise of considerable amount, were not utilized as intended.

We have recommended that Management stop immediately the withholding of loan amortizations in the salary payroll of municipal employees concerned which are due to private institutions pursuant to the provisions of Section 52 of General Appropriations Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2019 and Section 335 of RA 7160.
Management commented that the said loans was granted by the Philippine Postal Savings Bank which is considered as GOCC, however, it was recently bought by Overseas Filipino Bank, Inc. The Audit Team was informed that availing of loans   from said bank was already stopped.

We have recommended that management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the following reports and accounts within the prescribed period for timely review and verification thereof.
We also recommended that management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions in order to ensure the validity, completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions.
Management commented that they instructed the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Accounting Office Personnel on the timely submission of accounts and reports in compliance with Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06.
There was no comment of management on the foregoing audit observations and recommendations.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort to collect the delinquent market stall rentals from stallholders by adopting an effective collection strategy in order to generate more income which could be used in the implementation of development programs/projects beneficial to the constituents.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict adherence with the existing laws, rules and regulations on remittance of mandatory contributions/premiums to GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG by requiring the OIC-Municipal Accountant to conduct a detailed review and analysis of the pertaining to said transactions and if warranted, make the corresponding adjustments; and to cause the immediate remittance of the premiums/contributions to the government agencies concerned. This is to ensure that the members will enjoy the services and benefits offered by the said government institutions and also to avoid the interest, penalties and surcharges that may be imposed for non-compliance.
Management commented that audit recommendation shall be complied with. The OIC-Municipal Accountant shall be instructed to make the necessary detailed reviews, analysis and reconciliation and make the necessary and corresponding adjustments and cause the immediate remittance of the premiums/contributions to the concerned agencies.

We have recommended that Management stop the practice of charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues,  women’s empowerment, and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, and to charge these expenses to other programs of the LGU where deemed appropriate.  We also recommended that the GAD Focal Point determine and identify GAD related issues in the locality which should be the primary consideration in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.
Furthermore, we have recommended that Management ensure the preparation of GAD Accomplishment Report which should be submitted on January or February of the succeeding year to support and document the implementation of GAD- related projects, programs and activities as stated in the GAD Plan and Budget pursuant to the provisions of C.8 and 5.5 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.
Management commented that the audit recommendations were noted. They will  stop the practice of charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues, the promotion of women’s empowerment and gender equality from the GAD as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, and to charge these expenses instead to other programs of the LGU where deemed appropriate. In relation to this, the GAD Focal Point will also determine in the future GAD related issues in the locality which is necessary in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality. They will also endeavor to submit on time the GAD Accomplishment Report on January or February of the succeeding year in compliance with the provisions of C.8 and 5.5 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System to –
Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.
Management commented that the audit recommendations shall be complied with.  The GAD Focal Point System shall be required to establish or set up a GAD Database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation as required under Section 37 (D), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9710 and Section 41.B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No.
2013-01.
Also, management commented that the LGU has partially complied with the establishment of a GAD Database through the implementation of the Barangay Information Management System by the MPDO wherein Job Orders were hired to conduct Barangay-based enumeration of various records and information. A spreadsheet and Geographic Information System-linked database was developed. Sample/Initial output will be printed for your reference. However, data gathering or survey was stopped in compliance to AOM No. 2019-53 dated October 7, 2019 which trimmed down the number of Job Order Personnel due to huge amount of expenditures representing wages for Job Order Personnel assigned in various departments/offices. The concerned Enumerators/Job Order Personnel submitted their individual bi-monthly accomplishment report as basis for payment.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure to implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provision of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues which needs immediate attention and action. We also recommended to encourage the members of the LCPC to prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries and the objectives of the law creating the LCPC fund shall be fully attained.
Management commented that the disbursements of ₱106,510.66 for the conduct of free therapy sessions to children with disability (CWD) is appropriate and proper to be charged to LCPC based on the provision of services and programs that responds to the special needs, interest and children concerns, and that the therapy sessions included guidance and counseling and guidance to the children and their families.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 provided the allocation of one percent (1%) Internal Revenue Allotment for strengthening and implementation of programs, projects and activities of the Local Councils for the Protection of Children (LCPC) pursuant to  RA 9344.  In Section 1, it explicitly covers the different stages including children at risk and children in conflict with the law from prevention to rehabilitation and reintegration.  In view hereof, the expenditures for free therapy session for children with disability could be charged to other programs of the LGU under Health and GAD.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate 1 % of the IRA for the implementation of programs on child delinquency in compliance with the provision of Section 15 of Chapter I, Title III of R.A. 9344. We also recommended that formulation of programs and activities funded therefrom is geared towards child protection and ensure proper implementation of programs in order to prevent juvenile delinquency in the locality.
Management explained that the actual appropriation of the LGU for the LCPC was ₱3,986,000.00 which was more than ₱1,075,436.03 or 1% of IRA minimum allotment required by RA 9344, and not only ₱50,000.00.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
While Management explained that the actual appropriation of the LGU for the LCPC was ₱3,986,000.00, we sustain the audit observation on the ground that the LGU had only allocated ₱50,000.00 as per approved 2019 Annual Budget which is less than the 1% requirement.  The minimum mandatory requirement of 1% for LCPC per RA 9344 may also be increased pursuant to DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 to ensure the implementation of priority programs for children. In this regard, all LGUs are encouraged to outsourced funds or engage in partnership with private organizations, establishments and entities that could provide financial assistance for the implementation of LCPC programs, activities and projects. Likewise, the undertakings may also be charged against the 20% Development Fund of the LGUs.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱3,468,480.15 under the General Fund to the Special Turst Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.
Management commented the said unexpended LDRRMF was already transferred to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund account in compliance with the audit recommendation.

We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations, and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare and certify the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) submit through the  LDRRMO on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.
Management commented that the concerned municipal office/LDRRMO had prepared the monthly report on sources and utilization of LDRRMF, however, might had been remiss on the submission of the report. Management commented that the audit recommendation shall be complied with, to conform to the provisions of Sec. 5.1.4 and 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended the following:
	

a. That the Office of the Municipal Accountant give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the required bank reconciliation statements pursuant to Section 474 of RA No. 7160 and COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

b. That the cash books of the Municipal Treasurer be updated always, and the data reflected in these books be considered in the preparation of the bank reconciliation statements to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted;

c. Any error and reconciling item discovered in the reconciliation process be adjusted in the respective records which need correction/revision, to arrive at correct and reliable cash in bank balance.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will organize her department and will assign a personnel that will be responsible for the preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements.
observations.reason: Due to lack of manpower and considering the bank reconciliation is a very time consuming task, they cannot give priority on its preparation.

The maintenance of Property Cards for the PPE of the Municipality was not given importance and priority by the Inventory Custodian and Municipal Accountant. Nonetheless, the Municipality already appointed a Supply Officer to facilitate the PPE and Inventory management of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant compute the depreciation based on available documents especially for those recently acquired properties and make the necessary adjustment on the affected accounts. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will gather documents that will be the bases for computation of the PPEs depreciation.
observations.reason: The provision of depreciation for some of the Municipality’s PPE was not prioritized by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by requiring the Municipal Accountant to review thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies every month and that remittances must be based on the actual collection vis-à-vis payroll deductions. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyse transactions before recording in the books and update entries of both records. Further, assign personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant did not conduct detailed review of the previous records of the stated accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert extra effort to prepare updated bank reconciliation statements for all funds and to submit the same to COA for verification. It will be used as basis to ascertain and validate the balances recorded in the Financial Statements for CY 2016.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to comply with the herein stated audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Due to lack of manpower and considering the bank reconciliation is a very time consuming task, they cannot give priority on its preparation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:


a)	Strictly enforce the settlement of all unliquidated cash advances as of December 30,2016; 

b)	Stop the granting of additional cash advances to Accountable Officers, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled, to avoid accumulation of the same; and 

c)	Institute the appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those erring accountable officers/employees for continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Management promised to exert more efforts in the liquidation of various cash advances.
observations.reason: The Management explained that they already sent a demand letters to the Accountable Officers transferred to other LGU but AOs refused to receive them. Nonetheless, the Management promised to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated compliance to the audit recommendations offered in the previous audit reports which primarily dealt on the assurance of conducting physical count of LGU properties and submitting updated RPCPPE to the auditor concerned.
observations.managementAction: The Property Custodian still promised to exert more efforts to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: The Property Custodian, who is also the Municipal Treasurer, cannot give priority to the property custodianship, issuance and inventory-taking, due to the demanding nature of her responsibility as Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
Facilitate the creation of an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of  properties of the municipality. The actual count could be undertaken any month during the current  year, but it has to be updated at year-end. Correspondingly inventory report shall be prepared and submitted/furnished to appropriate offices one of which is the COA, and submitted not later than January 31 of each year.
Reconcile the PPE per inventory report with accounting records/reports. Any discrepancies shall be verified and adjusted accordingly.  Recipient of any missing PPEs be held accountable.
Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.
Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.
Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.
Make use of Invenory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper accountability and control.
During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal.
Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.
Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We have recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Assessor prepare and furnish the OIC-Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to record the purchases of supplies and materials by using the perpetual inventory method except for purchases out of the petty cash fund which is to be recorded as an outright expense. Issuance of supplies and materials shall be supported by a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which will be consolidated in a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI). It shall be the basis of the Accounting Unit to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize the monthly issuance of supplies and materials in the books of accounts.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive require periodic physical inventory of supplies or property.  Physical count of inventory items by type shall be conducted every six months and be reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). This shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
For other concerns on supply management and accounting thereof, please be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain individual subsidiary ledger for all accounts, as required under Sections 111 and 114 PD No. 1445. Exert diligent efforts to locate the necessary supporting documents to determine the exact age of the receivable accounts, to identify the government agencies and individuals or  entities obliged to liquidate and/or refund unutilized balance of fund transfers and to establish the validity and accuracy of the respective receivable account balances presented in the financial statements.
When these accounts are confirmed to be non-moving for more than five (5) years, these are considered to be dormant accounts. If the analysis or review of the accounts is not possible due to the absence of records and documents, the agency head concerned should request for write-off and or adjustment of account balances from the COA, supported by:
List of available records and extent of validation made on the accounts; and
Certification and reasons why the books of accounts/records, financial statements/schedules and supporting vouchers/documents cannot be located.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Assessor to exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership of the lots through a Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in the name of the Municipal Government of San Jacinto in compliance with the provisions of Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual in order to protect the interest of the Municipality for any adverse claims by third parties.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to transfer 75% of the local road account from the Registry of Public Infrastructure to the General Fund books which will form part of the Property, Plant and Equipment under Road Networks account in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-008.  We also recommend that the OIC-Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers for the Road Networks account to facilitate reconciliation of inventory of said account,  verify the journal entries made in the recording of Road Networks based on the percentage of Construction in Progress and make the necessary adjustments to properly record the correct account of completed projects which were taken up as Road Networks, and stop the practice of recording percentage of the balance of the Construction in Progress to ensure that the amount of said account is correctly presented in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Accounting Division verify the journal entries made relative to the Nego Cart, concreting of Farm to Market Road in various barangays, Construction of Water Pumping Station for Farming located in different barangays as well as the Construction of Multi-Purpose Drying Pavement and to make the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts for fair presentation of the Other Property, Plant and Equipment, Road Networks, Water Supply Systems and Other Infrastructure Project accounts in the financial statements.

We therefore reiterate our previous year’s recommendation that management strictly comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations pertaining to granting and liquidation of cash advances. It is further recommended that:
No additional Cash Advance shall be given to officials/employees unless the previous cash advances was first settled;
All cash advances shall be supported by an Accountant’s Certification that the requesting party has no existing unliquidated cash advances; and
Withhold the salaries of officials and employees whose cash advance remains unliquidated once efforts yield no encouraging results.

We have recommended that Management conduct physical inventory of all Property, Plant and Equipment to determine the actual insurable properties of the LGU.  We also recommend that Management submit updated inventory report of properties to the GSIS for appraisal purposes to determine the updated amount of premiums for the insurance of all insurable assets, and allocate additional budget for the payment of insurance premiums, if necessary.

we are reiterating our previous year’s audit recommendation that management adopt the Online Payroll System which is a time and cost efficient scheme of disbursing payroll to its employees. Likewise, it prevents possible risk such as robbery, hold-up and similar incidents. Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby encourage.

We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the procedures in the establishment replenishment cash advance granted under Petty Cash Fund in accordance with Section 4.3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 48 of Vol. I of MNGAS.

we have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant and ICO-Municipal Treasurer ensure that provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the DSWD are adhered to, which include compliance on proper and complete documentation, before effecting the payment.  Also, we also have recommended that management refrain from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit. The LGU should also formulate concrete basis/guidelines for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.
It is also  recommended that a separate Petty Cash Fund with a reasonable amount be set up to facilitate the payment of financial assistance for burial, transportation, medical assistance and other emergency needs with an amount not exceeding ₱3,000.00. Pursuant to the general policies of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008, these shall be paid in outright cash directly to the client. It will also eliminate numerous reimbursements of financial assistance.

we have recommended that the Members of the Sangguniang Bayan immediately stop the practice of granting financial assistance/donations to their constituents as this is not among their mandated duties and functions under Section 447 (a) of R.A. No. 7160. Appropriated funds for donations under the said office should be fully transferred to the MSWDO whose task is to implement such programs, projects and activities.
Also, we have recommended that the Accounting Office record the financial assistance granted to individuals by the Municipality under Donation account by the Municipality to present fairly the correct amount of the specific account in the financial statements.

We have recommended the creation of Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and designation of a focal person to carry out effectively the implementation of programs, projects and activities related to Solid Waste Management that is geared towards the preservation and protection of the environment and public health as required under R.A. No.  9003.  We also recommended that management involve the participation of the barangay officials in information dissemination on the importance of segregations of waste at source, and to forewarn them that incineration of solid waste  be avoided because it is prohibited by law.

We have recommended that the management ensure that the projects/programs/activities designed under the 20% Development Fund are implemented fully within the time frame. Optimal utilization of the 20% IRA is also recommended in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as Chairman of the Local School Board, strictly adhere with the rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of the SEF particularly for the construction and repair of school buildings and establishment of new extension classes or acquisition of books and instructional materials of students and teachers in public schools pursuant to DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 and 1A in order to ensure an effective educational development within the locality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims are duly supported with complete documentation in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445 before effecting payments to avoid issuance of notice of suspension/disallowance in audit.

We have recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its SEF in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to transfer the PCSO funds received to the General Fund and ensure that appropriations are provided therefor in the utilization thereof;
To submit copies of Memorandum of Agreement entered into between the LGU with PCSO for CY 2017 and CY 2018 indicating the category of programs for which its allocation or funds may be utilized and the reportorial requirements pertaining to its utilization as stated in Section 40 of the 2016 Revised IRR for the Small Town Lottery Operations of the PCSO.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to determine the cause of the negative balance of the Due from LGUs account and make the necessary adjustment in the books.  We also recommended that the OIC- Municipal Accountant ensure that transactions relative to the receivable account do not exceed the amount of receivables taken up in the books in order avoid the recurrence of negative balance of account in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Accounting Office to verify the journal entries to determine the correct adjusting journal entry in the books to reconcile reciprocal accounts.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all other concern strictly adhere to the policies/guidelines governing the hiring of Job Orders (JOs) and their entitlements. Management should stop from approving travel order of Job Orders to attend conference/seminars to prevent the LGU’s coffers being used to activities in which it could not benefit.
We further recommended the refund of the amount reimbursed to the JOs in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive create a committee to undertake a thorough study and evaluation on the actual workloads and manpower requirements of the Municipality for each Department/Office to maximize the services of Job Order Personnel, thus, minimize government spending. Based on the study and evaluation, trimmed down the number of JOs to what is really required and necessary in the operation of the Municipality in order to maximize the use of government funds.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer explain the incurrence of overdraft amounting to ₱ 5,101,476.00. Present approved supplemental budget, if any.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to process only those disbursement vouchers with complete documentation pursuant to the provision of Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.  We also have recommended that the Municipal Accountant should submit all the above-mentioned supporting documents to the audit team avoid suspension/ disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the LGU adopt the electronic or computerized system in assessment, collection and accounting which is a time and cost efficient scheme in processing transactions and in the preparation of reports. Likewise, it prevents errors and inconsistencies in processing and generating reports. Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, we also have recommended that computerization of government transaction be given importance, thus, a corresponding budget be appropriated for this purpose.

We have recommended that claims of Representation and Transportation Allowances be supported with the required documents which shall serve as the basis in determining whether the provisions of the afore-cited laws, rules and regulations were observed in granting RATA. The OIC-Municipal Accountant and ICO-Municipal Treasurer should ensure that claims for RATA are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment.

We have recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to record the purchases of supplies and materials by using the perpetual inventory method except for purchases out of the petty cash fund which is to be recorded as an outright expense. Issuance of supplies and materials shall be supported by a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which will be consolidated in a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI). It shall be the basis of the Accounting Unit to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize the monthly issuance of supplies and materials in the books of accounts.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive require periodic physical inventory of supplies or property.  Physical count of inventory items by type shall be conducted semestrally and reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). This shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
For other concerns on supply management and accounting thereof, please be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.

We have recommended that the Office of the Auditor be notified by ICO- Municipal Treasurer as “the official responsible for or in-charge of accepting deliveries of procured items” pursuant to No. 7.4 and No 7.7 of COA Circular No. 94-006 and Training Handbook on Property & Supply Management System to enable the COA Audit Team to conduct timely inspection to ascertain the correctness of quantity and propriety of specification and purchase description of items delivered.

We have recommended the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to strictly comply with Section 7 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184 and Updated Budget Operations Manual for Local Government Units in the preparation of APP and PPMP.  The BAC must carefully plan and schedule the agency’s procurement in order to manage its resources, produce economical result, and make the procurement procedures more efficient and practical.
Ensure that the prescribed format under DBM Circular Letter 2011-6 be strictly followed in the preparation of APP-CSE  to come up with a realistic budget in the procurement of common use supplies and equipment.

We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee prepare and submit to the Head of the Procuring Entity the Procurement and Monitoring Report and submit the same to the GPPB pursuant to IRR of RA 9184.
We further recommended that the BAC furnish us copy of the said report for verification and validation purposes on the status of the procurement process. It is also a useful tool for review of contracts related to the procurement of goods and infrastructure projects of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all department heads of the LGU to purchase common-use supplies and equipment at the Procurement Service pursuant to Administrative Order No. 17 s.  2011 and DBM Circular Letter No. 2011-6 to avail the most advantageous price for the government. When goods are to be purchased from local/private suppliers, ensure that these shall always be supported by an evidence of non-availability of stocks in the PS and shall be purchased at reasonable prices.

a. That the ICO-Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the above-mentioned provisions whereby all collections shall be deposited intact and daily or not later than the next banking day with the authorized depository bank to avoid possible loss or misuse of government funds.
b. That the ICO- Municipal Treasurer should ensure that all checks to be released to the payees/claimants must be properly acknowledged by affixing their signatures in the Check Register.
c. That the OIC-Municipal Accountant effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts on the stale checks in accordance with the provision of Section 97 of PD 1445 and Section 59 (3), Volume I of Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretary and OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents enumerated therein in order to complete the contract review and technical evaluation said project to avoid issuance of notice of suspension.

Fill-up vacant positions to optimize the utilization of approved budget for Personal Services so that funds shall be utilized for its intended purpose. Initially, require the Head of HRMO to publish the vacant positions in the newspaper as required by the Civil Service Commission, wherein a copy be furnished to this Office.
If the ICO’s or OIC’s of Departments concerned be found qualified for the position, they are encourage to apply and be considered for the position, considering that their present salary do not match their accountability and function.

We have recommended that management require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the following reports and accounts within the prescribed period for timely review and verification thereof.
We also recommended that management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions and to ensure validity, completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions.

We have recommended that Management requires the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of trial balance and bank reconciliation statements on the 20th day of the following month and submission of interim reports on the 20th day after the end of each quarter pursuant to Sections 70 and 73, Volume 1 of MNGAS and ensure that the necessary attachment to the trial balance be also submitted in compliance with the above-cited provision.
We reiterate our previous year’s audit recommendations that the management apply the civil remedies as provided under Section 174, Chapter IV of R.A. 7160.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the GSIS regulations remittance of monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of PhilHealth premiums.

We have recommended that Management stop from charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues, promote women’s empowerment, and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01; and to charge it to other programs of the LGU.  We also recommended that the GAD Focal Person assist in determining GAD related issues in the locality which will be considered in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate 1 % of the IRA for the implementation of programs on child delinquency in compliance with the provision of Section 15 of Chapter I, Title III of R.A. 9344. We also recommended that formulation of programs and activities funded therefrom is geared towards child protection and ensure proper implementation of programs in order to prevent juvenile delinquency in the locality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accoutant and ICO-Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱ 1,611,830.70 under the General Fund to the Special Turst Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.

We have recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Circular and revert back the unexpended balance of Trust Liabilities –DRRM for CY 2012 from Trust Fund to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund which could be used for other social services through enactment by the Local Sanggunian of the Municipality.

We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations, and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare and certify the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) submit through the  LDRRMO on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Advise the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions of the New Government Accounting System Manual for Local Government Units (NGAS-LGUs), COA Circular 2010-001 and IPSAS I in the submission of the complete set of financial reports on a monthly, quarterly and annual basis; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over the record keeping of the Municipality’s transactions and to devise and implement strategies that will help the Accounting Office in the performance of its duties and responsibilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by requiring the Municipal Accountant to review thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies during the month and that remittances must be based on the actual payroll deductions. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully analyze every transaction before recording in the books. Assign personnel to review the entries made to each subsidiary account with negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Create a General Services Officer (GSO) position that will discharge on a full time basis the property custodianship functions and the receipt, issuance and recording of the procurement and issuance of supplies and property, maintain updated Property Cards per category on all property, plant and equipment as recorded in the list of Property, Plant and Equipment of accounting records and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the RPCPPE;
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain property ledger cards consistent with their accounting records; and
Maintain and renew all Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment every three years for the signature of the holder of each item listed in the prepared RPCPPE or based on the old Memorandum Receipts on file.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation based on available documents especially for those recently acquired properties and make the necessary adjustment on the affected accounts. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the depreciable properties of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 51 of the MNGAS for LGUs on the proper recording and reporting of transactions which requires that all regular purchases of supplies must be booked through the inventory account whether it is consumed or not within the accounting period and maintenance of  subsidiary ledger cards;
Direct each Department Head to conduct an inventory to establish the existence of the office supplies, medicines and other items/inventories for stock to serve as basis for reconciliation of the same with the reports of the accounting; and
Create a General Services Officer (GSO) position, if warranted, that will discharge on a full time basis the property custodianship functions and the receipt, issuance and recording of the procurement and issuance of supplies and property; maintain stock cards per category on all inventories as recorded in the list of Inventory accounting records; and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI).

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the officials and employees of the Municipality to submit promptly their claims or reports to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for review, verification and recording purposes;
Require the Municipal Accountant to fast track the submission of the said accounts complete with their respective supporting documents and to evaluate their current system and existing manpower for any necessary improvement or augmentation to prevent the further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding financial years; and
Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected contracts and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for a timely and complete conduct of Auditorial and technical review.

We recommended that the Municipality prioritize the corrections of the 10-year SWM Plan and submit it to the EMB Regional Office V and NSWC for approval, as required under Section 16 of RA 9003 to ensure the efficient management of solid waste generated within its jurisdiction.
We recommended that the Municipality comply with the provisions of RA 9003 and DENR AO No. 9 and issue an ordinance for the closure and rehabilitation of the Municipality’s existing controlled dumpsite located at Brgy. Burgos, San Jacinto, Masbate.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor evaluate the composition of the Municipality Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) and look for possible members that could qualify to complete the lineup.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare monthly the Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and instruct the LDRRM Officer to submit a copy thereof to the office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, pursuant to the procedures of the forecited Circular, to facilitate verification thereof.

Guided by the dispositive portion of the “PDAF Decision,” we therefore reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the unexpended balance of PDAF with a total amount of ₱4,297,623.97 be returned to the Bureau of Treasury so that the funds could be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund and augment future legitimate funding requirement of the government.
observations.managementAction: Unexpended balance of PDAF has been returned to the Bureau of Treasury
observations.reason: The balance of PDAF amounting to ₱4,287,112.39 was refunded to the Bureau of Treasury thru Deposit Slips all dated May 25,2018. However, the remaining balance amounting to   P 10,511.58 was not refunded to the Bureau of Treasury because the amount pertains to PCF balance (balance of ₱700,000.00).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Division verify the journal entries made relative to the recognition of completed projects in the books of accounts and make the necessary correcting entries for fair presentation of the PPE accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason: Verified the account but the completed projects were not yet recorded in the books of accounts under Trust fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the local government unit transfer all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund and the Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund” (account number 2-04-01-020)  in the Trust Fund book.
observations.managementAction: Instructed the Accounting Office to prepare DVs regarding the transfer of the unobligated LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund
observations.reason: The unexpended balance of LDRRMF-MOOE for CY 2017 was not yet transferred to the Special Trust account under Trust fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to certify only the loan applications of employees whose salary could accommodate the monthly loan amortization and still have a take home pay of not less than the amount specified in the General Provisions of the General Appropriation Act. 
      Effective CY 2018, the minimum take home pay is increased to ₱5,000.00, so please be guided accordingly.
observations.managementAction: Complied

observations.recommendation:
To address the above concerns in the payment of travelling expenses and to avoid suspensions and disallowance in the future, the following measures are hereby recommended:
a.   Be guided accordingly by the rules and regulations   provided in Executive Order No. 298 in the payment/ reimbursements of travelling expenses.
b. Ensure that the claims are supported with original documents except for those documents that were approved or certified by group.
c. Take advantage of the new interactive technologies such as internet, cellphones, telephones, e-mails and fax machines to convey communications and to follow up official matters.  These are the fastest way to transmit communication. 
d.   In the preparation of a travel order, the last entry must be followed by the word “Nothing Follows” to prevent additional entry of dates of travel.  Any modification shall be countersigned by the approving official.
e.    Ensure that dates of travel are consistent with the Driver’s Trip Ticket, Travel Order and Certificate of Appearance.
f.    The Head of the Agency and the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office have to exercise judgment and discretion in determining the reasonableness of the claims. 
      Please be reminded that the nature of travel shall cover only those that are urgent and extremely necessary and will involve the minimum expenditure and are beneficial to the agency concerned and/ or the country (Handbook on Government Expenditures-National, pp. 97).
observations.managementAction:
Management commented that the audit recommendations shall be observed  pursuant to Executive Order No. 298 in connection with the payment /reimbursement of travelling expenses.  The Audit Team was also informed that  Management will exercise sound judgment and discretion in the determination of reasonable claims.









Instructed claimants to provide the documents needed
observations.reason: Computation of traveling expenses of municipal employees, except the department heads, were already based on the rules and regulations of EO 298,,except the department heads

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to refrain from granting Honorarium to COMELEC Election Officer and BIR Collection Agent pursuant to DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines No. 98-1 dated March 23, 1998, to avoid disallowance in audit. 
      In addition, we recommended that Management cause the refund of the total amount of honoraria granted to the COMELEC Election Officer and BIR Collection Agent which is not in accordance with afore cited DBM guidelines.
observations.managementAction: Complied

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management limit the hiring of experts to its actual need of consultancy services taking into consideration the specific work requiring highly specialized or technical expertise in a field of special knowledge or training which cannot be provided by the regular staff of the Municipality. 
      We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the submission of monthly accomplishment report from the Consultants to support the claim of honorarium.
observations.managementAction: As recommended, we have limited the hiring of consultants and advised the Municipal Accountant to require the submission of monthly report from the consultants
The Management will send demand letters and enforce settlement of the undeposited collections.
observations.reason:
The Management started settlement of this account. During 2018, the Municipality adjusted a total amount of ₱375,532.20 resulted to a lower balance of Cash in Vault account.

The collectors remit collections to the Municipal Treasurer. Maintenance of subsidiary ledgers for accountable officers was not prioritize by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal officials and employees concerned to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected contracts and their supporting documents to COA for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review, the results of which will be used in post-audit of disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The BAC will submit the contracts of all PPAs of the Municipality together with their complete supporting documents enumerated in Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.
observations.reason:
The BAC Chairman submits contracts of some PPAs with incomplete supporting documents.

See Observation No. 9

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Office of the Municipal Accountant to fast track the submission of the said accounts complete with their respective supporting documents to facilitate timely review and verification of the LGUs CY 2017 financial transactions. This is without prejudice to our further audit action regarding the suspensions/disallowances that may be issued on the subject disbursements. It is also suggested that they evaluate their current system and existing manpower for any necessary improvement or augmentation to prevent the further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding financial years.

We also reminded the Management and the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended to require the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to provide the Office of the Auditor at the beginning of the year a list of all on-going and to be implemented PPAs during the year. Further, a monthly monitoring report on the status of the projects must be submitted. Likewise, the Municipal Engineer must see to it that verification of the projects’ status as reported in its monthly report in accordance with the frequency of verification provided in Item 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 be timely requested and the necessary documents be submitted to facilitate the inspection by COA-TAS.
observations.reason: The Municipal Planning and Development Officer submits quarterly reports of all on-going and to be implemented projects of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare monthly the Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and instruct the LDRRM Officer to submit a copy thereof to the office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, pursuant to the procedures of the forecited Circular, to facilitate verification thereof.
